03/17/2026
Altius
Sonicator v2.1 adjustments
Last week we got some feedback from Seno for the v2.1 sonicator module
Here are the feedbacks and issues I was focusing this week:
Reduce preload force sensitivity
Last week, Seno found out that the preload force is really sensitive to the position change; 1mm displacement causes the preload to be reduced up to 500gr.
This week I applied some changes to fix this issue:
Switch the leaf spring to smaller width (from 13mm to 5mm)
Switch the compression spring to UR6-30, smaller k (from 0.5N/mm to 0.3N/mm)
Increase preload depth from 2mm to 3mm. This is needed to ensure good contact of the horn. Although the depth is increased, it was compensated by the reduction in spring constant both from the leaf spring and the compression spring.
Here are the preload test results after we applied those changes.

No
	Condition
	Load (gr)
	Screw extrusion (mm)
	Measurement video link
	
1
	No comp spring (leaf spring only)
	120
	12

	600

	link

The initial preload of the leaf spring is 120gr. I think this is small enough, since the preload depth is 3mm, which means that the leaf spring constant is only 40gr/mm. Also, with this leaf spring the assembly looks sturdy enough. Currently, I still want to test the leaf spring’s durability by keeping it in the bent position and will look for any deformation after some period of time. Picture below showing the transducer movement with leaf spring only, it has that “jiggly” movement once the load is removed.

We can easily set the preload force to 500-900gr. Now, the preload force sensitivity is +/-160gr/mm of displacement, which I think is low enough. The problem with lowering the spring constant again is that we’ll have a lower ceiling of the preload force. I think the current 500-900gr is ideal for our use.


Fix x-axis adjustability issue
The other issue we had is the difficulty to adjust the module in x axis. This will create problems since the horn could become not centered with the vial.
Previously, we actually designed the base plate to be adjustable by +/-1mm by using a slot for the fastener and pin hole. But this was proven to be hard to adjust if the pins and screws are not aligned.
This week, I tried to add adjustability from the leaf spring base, changing the fastener holes with slots. This solved the issue, but redesign in 3 parts is needed. 

Fix wedge design
One final thing to fix is the wedge plate design. Previously we were playing with wedge geometry to solve the preload sensitivity issue, but now since that issue is solved, we can lock the wedge design.
What we want for the wedge design is to avoid overconstraint which affects the transducer movement, we also want to keep the sonication position in motorpos 12mm.
Still on progress.
All these feedbacks are necessary for our v3 sonicator module build, because it uses the same mechanism. We will apply some good feedback to the v3 design.

In the latest melt curve where both hot and cold blocks were set to 60C, the gradient across the voxel was pretty bad by the end of the melt curve for IS6110 (the center channel):

Because the IS6110 assay melts at a temperature at 90C or above, and the hot and cold blocks are so close to the voxel, that 30C temp difference might be too much.  This will be less of a problem for the real probe melt since it should melt at a lower temperature of ~75C.  But we might still want to choose a hot and cold block temperature to be near to the melt temp.  Hopefully we will be able to ramp the hot side block at the same time as the top block, and then that will reduce the gradient on one side.  And if really necessary, we can move the cold block (or cut it) a little away from the voxel.

Kabir
The latest melt curves with the normalizing looked great. I put all three melt peaks on the same graph so it is easier to compare: 

Here are a few suggestions for future tests: 
Since the human target and the RIF probes will have a much lower melt we might need to start at 60C (even though starting at 70C might be better for the IS6110 assay). 
For some reason the fluorescence is already dropping when the melt data is starting. Maybe because the temperature of the liquid is increasing in the voxel even before the top heater turns on.  Ideally the temperature is steady before we start heating the top heater, so maybe we want a 1 minute pause with the liquid in the voxel before heating the top heater. 
There is a temperature gradient in the voxel with the liquid on the sides cooler than the center which you can see in the fluorescence.  Maybe the heat is conducting through the liquid from the bottom heaters.  We could try also ramping the bottom hot side heater as we ramp the top heater to reduce this gradient.  I don’t think we can change the bottom cold side heater because that will probably shift the slugs.  
Also to reduce the effect of the temperature gradient, it might help to measure a smaller section of the liquid in the middle of the voxel, which will probably make the peak sharper.

Kabir
Melt Curve Proof of Concept
Here is the setup I used to do melt curve testing:

It used a separate heater that was 20mmx20mm connected to a 3d printer board to raise the temperature from the top of the cartridge over the voxel.  You can use whatever heater is easiest to control. I don’t think it matters too much as long as it has ok contact with the top of the cartridge.  The clamp force doesn’t have to be high.  

Here is a video of what a melt curve looks like to compare the IS6110 and Human RNAseP amplicons with Evagreen: 
Generally the process that worked best for me was:
After PCR set the cold block and the top block to 60C.  Set the hot block to 95C.  
Move slugs onto the hot block at 95C, and pause for 30 seconds. 
Set the hot block to 60C while the slugs are still on it.  This cools the liquid slowly and helps anneal the double stranded DNA properly. If we drop the temperature very fast (like moving to the cold block), then the melt curve doesn’t look as clean. 
After all 3 blocks are at ~60C.  Move the liquid to the voxel and then give it a few seconds to stabilize
Then set the top block to 105C.  Don’t move the liquid at all, if there is any movement, the slugs touch different temperatures and causes non-smooth temperature changes
Continuously measure the fluorescence in the voxel and we should see the fluorescence go down when the DNA denatures. 

This is an example of the result with evagreen and the IS6110 and Human amplicons: 
The jumps in fluorescence are when I adjusted the slug position.  After this test I realized that the cold block PID was causing the liquid movement.  The cold block temperature was oscillating which moved the slugs.  So after fixing that the slugs stayed in one place more consistently and then I didn’t have to move the motors to readjust it.  


This is an example of the SNP450 FAM probe melt (no Evagreen).  The fluorescence drop is not as significant, but with some processing it makes a pretty clear melt curve:

The google sheet for these tests is here: 
We can always try different sequences once we have a heater on top.  Also the heater probably shouldn’t extend too far onto the cold block, because if the heater heats the air between the slugs and the pins, it will shift the slugs.  The heater can extend onto the hot block, and may even be useful for PCR cycling in the future to help heat the top side of the slugs during hot start or PCR.

Research on Accoustic Emmission (AE)



I remake the SW. The dBFS unit and %FS unit FS mean a full scale which actually represents the mV spike from the mic, but because the laptop cannot know the mV from the amplifier , %FS is used as a substitute which represents the gain. When we move the physical knob gain from the amplifier the %FS also increases. dBFS is also put on instead of dB to make the value not sensitive to db changing on FFT.  



We found any currents leak from the ground cable which sign by existing of trimmer signal and when we disconnect the ground the trimmer frequency on AE is gone.Both on oscilloscope & Ultrasonic mic detect same waveform which we conclude the transducer really vibrate when board is on stand by mode. We currently don't know the effect on the system. Based on SW , its only -36dbFS and 1%FS shock for standby meanwhile the sonication condition on -17 dbFS and 20%FS.  



This ultrasonic mic & ground-current leak is also useful to detect the resonance frequency of the transducer , where we could spot the resonance frequency by shifting the frequency and search the higher %FS and dBFS. On video when we were out of sight we got 2%FS and when we were on the sweet spot we had >10%FS. We could check the transducer frequency quickly w/o change the special impedance bridge circuit.

   

After we confirmed the transducer physical vibration identification to the mic I found a 1st harmonic on 72-75 kHz on the PZTOD35-32SO transducer. This harmonic component only strongly detected when mic axis aligning with horn .We have a few suspicious why the transducer have 2 component frequency, its maybe we have connection on horn-to converter and we still have little gap non flat which caused any harmonic frequency on those. We would visit the other transducer (PZTOD25-37SO) to compare the spectral.  



When the trimmer is put on near natural frequency it's the harmonic also leveraging. 



This tool is also useful to scan the mechanical anti resonance which is signed by near-zero dbFS/%FS on specific frequency. We found that PZTOD35-32SO has anti resonance on 39kHz and 33kHz. 



The condition when sonication in Air, the transducer carries many frequencies. The Fundamental frequency still has strongness at -17dbFS &  >20%FS.

Based on the checking capability of ultrasonic mic, Interesting to check any case like
dbFS & %FS & Antiresonance consistency for Transducer to Transducer and Board to Board 
Prestress of transducer Variability to dbFS & %FS
Matching Network on circuit variant to dbFS & %FS

V2 reliability and lifetime testing

We were talking in FUSA today about the reliability of the instruments. We want to do instrument lifetime testing with the V2 instrument after we build and validate the V3. Here’s what we’d like to do:
Vials filled with buffer
Cut off the needle on the cartridge so that the vial does not get pierced. We don’t want any liquid in the cartridge.
Create a “lifetime testing” sequence in the instrument that does the normal sonication, heat killing, and thermocycling. The sequence should repeat maybe 3 or 5 times, then stop so that an engineer can remove the cartridge and put in a new one.
Every 50 runs, do a real full-sequence PCR run to check the performance. Record the data and note any issues or anomalies.
The engineer should also check the instrument for worn out parts, like heater gap pads, after every PCR test.

Again, we don’t need to do this lifetime testing until the V3 instrument is working and one of the V2 instruments is no longer needed for more important testing. This is a low priority.

Team scrum on Friday 
Direction 
New plan for TS006 Sonic subsystem 
After debugging unit 006 and completing eight successful repetition tests, we identified a failure on Friday (liquid hanging during sonication). Based on this finding, we concluded that 006 carries a high operational risk for Saturday testing. As a short-term mitigation, we agreed to remove 006 from the Saturday test lineup.
Moving forward, our plan is to further improve 006 and then subsequently retrofit systems 003, 004, and 005 to V2. The primary issue in V2 is a design misalignment—specifically, the adjustment range is too narrow to accommodate the variability and failure modes observed during debugging. This limitation reduces robustness when facing real operational conditions. Therefore, we initiated a short sprint retrofit, involving both the design and manufacturing teams, to reassess the overall architecture, critical interfaces, and mechanical tolerances to ensure better alignment and adjustability.
From the electronics side, we are also exploring options to increase current delivery to the transducer in order to boost amplitude and improve vial retracting reliability. However, this approach is still at the proof-of-concept (PoC) stage. If the risk level remains significant or introduces new uncertainties, we will reconsider not including this modification in the
At the same time, we are emphasizing a more robust design approach to ensure sufficient adjustment margin, manufacturability, and tolerance control. The goal is to prevent recurring variability issues and to better manage the wide variability spread observed in instrument 006.

Dika
LED Adjuster Board Adjustment

This was conducted after we found an issue with the cable installation last week.  The main issues are the small spaces that cause the cable to bend tightly against the optic board, and the EE team find it difficult to check whether the board is still on, because there’s no exposed wire/path available.


The idea is to widen the board and adjust the port position to allow the cable to bend over a larger area. We also provided a small buffer area (1.8 mm) behind the port so that the EE team can create an exposed path to make checking easier.

This adjustment to the board affects nearby components, such as the cartridge aligner.  The adjustment includes a wider square to provide deeper board ports and a larger fillet (r= 3mm) for a seamless transition when the user inputs the cartridge.

QnA From project Group 
Q1. What is the function of the grey and black 3D printed parts behind the transducer?
The 3D printed parts introduce controlled mechanical compliance into the mounting system. They replace the rigid behavior of the previous aluminum rings. Their main function is to improve strain distribution during horn–vial contact. By reducing over-constraint, resonance becomes more stable under load. This helps equalize back EMF response across the stack. As a result, harmonic distortion decreases. The excitation system does not need to overcompensate. Consequently, Vrms drops from approximately 1kV to 700–800V.
Q2. What is the function of the Teflon shim?
The Teflon shim is not intended to increase transducer height. It is also not designed to improve bearing smoothness. Its primary role is to stabilize the mechanical interface. It helps reduce micro-scale clearance and friction variation. Its helping to make the 3D printed parts work in proper regime
Q3. Is replacing the two aluminum rings with 3D printed parts safe for continued operation?
Based on bench jig testing, the 3D printed parts have shown stable performance. They rarely require replacement during normal operation. Current Vrms remain within the safe 700–800V range. Harmonic levels are controlled compared to the rigid configuration. Short-term operation under this setup is acceptable. However, long-term durability should still be monitored. A machined spare solution is recommended for robustness. For this week, the configuration is considered operationally safe.
Q4. Why is simply loosening the clamp or preload bolts not recommended?
Loosening bolts does not create a self-align mechanism. It only reduces clamping force without adding elasticity. There is no controlled compliance in that condition. Micro-scale clearance can remain between interfaces. During ultrasonic vibration, this may cause micro-shift. Such movement can disturb resonance stability. That instability may increase harmonic distortion. Therefore, controlled compliance is safer than uncontrolled looseness.

Weekly Technical Update
1. Sonication Subsystem Performance – New Flexure
Tested sonication using the new flexure width configuration (100 g / 1 mm).
In vial temperature reached almost ~60 °C, indicating sufficient energy delivery.
No abnormal behavior observed during quick scanning; early indication suggests stable operation.
2. Device 006 – Sonication Sudden Shutdown Investigation
Reviewed and clarified Bowo’s journal entry by comment regarding the sudden shutdown in the Device 006 sonicator subsystem (See the Bowo posted).
For Progressive RCA testing, a complete package (sonic board + transducer) is required.
The transducer has already been removed; currently awaiting the sonic board.
If Device 006 becomes available (no urgent use), might to:
Remove the existing sonic board.
Replace it with a new board.
Pair the old board with the old transducer for root-cause investigation on jigbench.
Objective: ensure no underlying robustness issue is overlooked and capture any improvement opportunities early.
3. PCR Lysis Window – Concept Development
Currently reconceptualizing the PCR lysis window as the safe operating region where physical conditions enable effective cell lysis without compromising PCR performance.
This window is intended to function as a gate for evaluating acceptable sonication subsystems from physical criteria (Heat and Acoustic emission) as a gate before PCR test (Useful for Bench& production QC or quick quality scanning).
The region is governed by cavitation mechanics—particularly bubble collapse—which can be monitored through thermal response and acoustic emission.
Immediate focus is to characterize these physical signatures and confirm consistent, repeatable emissions under controlled conditions.
Once validated, the mapped operating region will serve as a physical benchmark during bench testing.
Final validation will correlate this region with PCR outcomes to safe conditions forming a mapping Physical properties to PCR safe zone
Ongoing activity: reviewing literature to correlate high-speed visual observations of bubble collapse with corresponding acoustic and thermal emission data from a few sources for international paper.  Link  bublecolapse video from paper

Kabir
We talked about an improvement to the channel between the vial and the junction by making the volume higher:

This should help for a few reasons:
During vial piercing the liquid won’t hit the junction, so then we can add counter pressure with the PCR pins if we want to, without risking bubbles in the liquid
We don’t need to let the vial cool down as much before piercing because prepriming won’t hit the junction
After the slugs are created it will be easier to retract the liquid far away from the junction, and that will reduce the chance of liquid hitting the junction during PCR cycling
Bulging silicone on the vent cap will be less likely to cause a failure because the liquid will be further from the junction during PCR so if it doesn’t retract as far as expected, it will still be ok. 
But there are some potential risks:
Will it cause more dead volume?  
We will have to push the vial silicone down more to prime the liquid to the junction.  Will we get close to stretching the silicone too far?  

How much volume do we need to add to that channel to prevent the liquid from hitting the junction during piercing, with some safety factor?  
If we did want to modify the shallow channel mold insert, would we be able to do it in an adjustable way?  Maybe we could add an insert in the red outlined area and then machine different volume channels out of it?

Dika
Flexure measurement:

This test was conducted to observe the behaviour of the locking flexure of the main door under a certain amount of displacement. The testing setup utilises a milling screw bed to ensure linear motion and prevent external uncertainties such as out-of-plane motion. The test involves placing a metal rod in the collet and positioning the gauge at the end of the flexure. This side is pulled by retracting the vice. Once the desired point is reached, the moving tip is removed and returned to the initial position. This allows us to determine whether it is still in the plastic or elastic area by measuring the return position. Several distances were proposed to be measured in 2 mm intervals. The results showed that the flexure started to reach the plastic stage after reaching an initial amplitude of 14 mm, and fractured after that.

Sonic preload force measurements
Today, after I retrofitted the sonicator module with the revised position (preload pos reduced from 3 to 1.5mm), I took some force measurements in different configurations.
From this experiment, I also identified the cause of the measurement issue we had earlier.
In previous tests, we’re struggling to increase the preload force by adjusting the compression spring screw.
This actually happened because I decided to modify and reduce the slope depth in the wedge plate. By doing this, the new shallower wedge acted as the stopper, so no matter how hard we set the preload screw, the force at the tip of the horn wouldn't increase.
Old design with deep slope (left), and new design with shallow slope (right). I did this redesign to prevent the horn from scratching the vial during piercing. But now, I think I should think of better design/geometry of the slope to prevent both issues.

Eventually in this test we ignored the wedge plate and did the test without it.
This is the measurement result
As control I set the zero pos of the measurement to have 250gr of preload.
First, I measured the force without the compression spring. I also added measurements with shimming added between the horn and the force gauge. This measurement is useful to understand the force change with slight shift in position, in this case I used a 0.7mm shim plate.
Next, I tested 2 types of spring that we have with different k factors (0.98 & 0.3 N/mm).
What we found here is that the measurement results don't really match with the calculation.
For example for the 4mm deflection of the spring K=0.98 N/mm, the force increase should be:
F=2.k.x, F=2.0,98.4, F=7.84N / 784gr
But what we got is only half of that, around 325gr.
We have checked the force gauge, and measured the individual spring, but everything looks normal.
Currently we’re still investigating this, one suspect is that it might be caused by the friction in the system.

Horn-Vial Coupling  Force Checking tools Evaluation 




Requirement condition
Contact point absolute location on horn & vial represented by load cell (along measurement)
Total avg Force repeatability 

Symptoms related number 1 requirements 

We found the loadcell dont have expected response when we push on  loadcell its only change around +- 6gr when horn push the load cell (we cannot confirm the visual because cover by vial heater) 
Based on our identification, we found that the load cell mounting allows rolling motion. This condition can disturb the load cell readings because part of the transducer’s push input energy is dissipated into unintended kinematic movement rather than being fully converted into strain on the load cell.
 minute 0:27
In addition, during calibration we removed the load cell package from its actual cartridge’s mounting configuration. As a result, the calibration setup did not represent the real operating condition of the tool. The material behavior observed during calibration therefore does not accurately reflect the behavior during actual tool usage.

Fixing Recommendations for Requirement #1
Mechanical Fix (Mounting Design)We found that contact between the load cell mounting cap and the vial heater creates a pivot point that enables rolling motion. Ideally, the mounting cap should not touch the vial heater board. Eliminating this contact will significantly reduce rolling behavior and improve force transmission to the load cell. ⇒ proven

Calibration Method ImprovementThe calibration condition should closely match actual tool usage. Calibration should be performed with the tool installed in the cartridge, allowing real strain–tilting–kinematic energy sharing to occur. This approach will ensure that the measured force more accurately represents real operating conditions and improves overall force measurement accuracy.

Symptoms related number 2 requirements 

After we add spacer 1mm , which is the make the contact point of horn and tip closer, the load cell could respond to the tip sonicator.  As Dika writes below . the repeatability is better than V2 tools which have under 40g variability.

Here the potential process comparison which could use for cut foil, 


Method
	Aspect
	Key Concerns / Findings
	Technical Impact
	Economic / Operational Notes
	
Laser Cutting
	Z-Axis Positioning
	Capacitive sensor may fail to detect 0.035 mm foil thickness, causing Z to pass through material
	Risk of head crashing or incomplete cutting
	Requires validation trial (Z-movement only)
	

	Gas-Assisted Cutting
	Assist gas pressure may lift or scatter thin foil, especially small parts
	Poor cut quality, part displacement
	High scrap risk
	

	Safe Setup Option
	Sandwich setup using tape + sacrificial plate (0.1–0.3 mm)
	Improves Z stability and cut consistency
	Additional material cost
	

	Thermal Impact
	FEM simulation (Fenics) shows ~200°C at X = 2 mm
	Potential adhesive degradation
	Depends on adhesive thermal tolerance
	

	Heating Duration
	Total cut time < 1 s; local exposure ~0.01 s
	Short duration reduces risk
	Still requires validation
	

	Thermal Gradient
	600–200°C gradient over ~0.03 s
	Possible adhesive damage
	≥2 mm sacrificial layer may be needed
	

	Next Validation Steps
	Z-movement trial without laser firing
	Confirms “Z through-away” risk
	Requires PE & Production alignment
	

	
	Cutting quality trial
	Evaluate burn marks and wrinkles
	—
	

	Laser Conclusion
	Technically feasible but complex
	High mechanical and thermal risk
	Not cost-effective
	
Sticker Cutting (Drag Knife)
	Cutting Method
	Dragging motion designed for vinyl
	Aluminum foil stiffness may resist cut
	Wrinkle formation likely
	

	Worst-Case Risk
	Blade unable to cut aluminum
	Incomplete cut
	Scrap & rework
	

	Material Holding
	Requires additional adhesive layer
	Added process complexity
	Extra cost
	

	Thermal Safety
	Cold cutting (no heat input)
	Adhesive fully protected
	Major advantage
	

	Potential Improvement
	Plunge / oscillating cutting
	100 Hz–20 kHz localized shear
	Requires R&D
	

	Sticker Cutting Conclusion
	Thermally safe but mechanically risky
	Uncertain cut reliability
	Needs method modification
	
Punching
	Tooling Requirement
	Dedicated punch & die required
	Tooling design needed
	Upfront investment
	

	Tool Material
	Aluminum workpiece demands steel tools
	Surface hardening required
	Improves durability
	

	Repeatability
	Highly consistent for mass production
	Stable quality
	Low unit cost at volume
	

	Punching Conclusion
	Most robust process
	No thermal risk
	Economically favorable long-term
	

Here for detail for each process : 


Foil by Laser opportunity  

Rino (Laser Leader) take the concern if we cutting on 0.035mm lasercut : 
The cutting on 0.035 mm would be caused the Z positioning would be through away the material because commonly the machine have capacitive sensor and make the Z potentially not stop on top of material 
This machine have gas assisted which have pressure on material even we clamp on side for foil, he afraid , for small things like our foil seal product, would be fly spreading caused bad cutting
Safely solution we need sacrifice for plate so we do sandwich setup by tape on plate(around 0.1mm or 0.3 mm) and foil, the rest of works just ensure the cutting is neat and little have wrinkle burn (But from economic side is more expensive because of additional for sacrifice )
Concern from functional side 
I notice the cutting temperature could harmful for adhesive, but based on my simulation by Fenics (open source python based FEM) , 




On this heat transfer simulation, we need pay attention heating material, on T=200 deg celsius, on pos X =2mm , 

This 200 deg celsius is potentially harmful for adhesive. And also need to notice the cutting time is assumed. The total assumed timing per part cut is only under one second , which is maybe per shoot only under 0,010 second per local point.

So the solution is put 2mm sacrifice if only that 600-200 C gradient on 0.030 second heating is harmful for adhesive quality.
 
The path if going with laser 
Because we need to trial on machine, we need consolidate with PE and Production if we still want retaining this plan  here the possibility path to pursue
Doing trial with rigid jig and no shoot just movement only on Z pos on machine to confirm the “Z through away” concern
Doing trial and error on cutting quality 
For sacrificing things , I think it's a bad idea from the economical side. 

Foil by Cutting sticker 

Engineering concerns
The cutting strike use dragging method to cut vinyl typical material its might caused : 
1. Wrinkle on edge because the foil seem like hard aluminum evenly on 0.035mm 
2. The worst case we have unable to cut the aluminum 
3. we need additional adhesive layer to hold the 0.035mm aluminium sheet 
But this cutting is included in the cold cutting group which is safe for our foil layer. 

Might We need modify the dragging method to plunge feed method maybe start from 100 Hz TO 20Khz Cutting for localized shear 



Foil by Punch 

We need punch and dies which are suitable for our product size. 
Maybe Machining could be set up on that because the workpiece is aluminum , we need do a surface harden on punch and die to make its more suitable for punching every time 
We need using at least steel material to doing punch & dies machining


Recommendation & Direction from Engineering Perspective
From my perspective, if the heat seal product design is still subject to change, the sticker cutting method with a modified plunger-feed mechanism should be selected as the short-term solution.
This approach provides higher flexibility during the design iteration phase and avoids thermal risks to the adhesive layer, as it belongs to the cold-cutting process category.
However, for the long-term and stable production phase, developing a dedicated punch-and-die tooling is the preferred solution. Punching offers superior repeatability, consistent quality, and eliminates both thermal and Z-positioning risks associated with laser cutting.
From an R&D standpoint, the development time required for punch-and-die tooling is comparable to the time needed to further refine and validate the modified cutting-sticker mechanism. Therefore, it is recommended to start punch-and-die tooling development in parallel, while using the modified sticker cutting method as a temporary solution during the design-freeze period.

Kabir
We think the main reason that the 85C preheat sequence had no amplification is not really because of the 85C damaging the reagents.  It is more related to the amount of time that the reagents spend at 60C.   In the 85C sequence, the reagents spend more than 2 minutes at 60C.  During this time, the polymerase is very active and is creating primer dimers and other non-specific reactions with the primers that end up adding nucleotides to the primers.  This is because 60C is low enough that the primers can sometimes bind to the “wrong” things, and there is not much of the “right” template at the beginning. Even at a 10E6 gblock template concentration, that is nothing compared to the 10E11 primer concentration, so if the primers have time to bind to each other at a temperature that allows the primer dimers, they will.  And those new nucleotides make it so the primers no longer fit with the correct gblock sequence.  So then we don’t amplify the correct sequence that contains the probe which gives us a signal.  

Sean has confirmed this effect with this experiment: 

The fTaq has no probe amplification when we cool down slowly from 85C to 60C (green lines in graph on left).  But in the same experiment with Evagreen there is a very early amplification (purple lines in graph on the right).  This is because Evagreen measures any amplification including primer dimers, so we can see that the primers get used up so early that there are none left for the proper gblock amplification.  

So this is why we want to spend as little time at 60C (or below 85C) as possible, especially at the beginning of the experiment.  We should never dwell with the full reagents on the cold block at <85C for more than 20s (and even 20s might be too long for cycling in the early cycles).  This is why we want a sequence where we mix a high temperature where the primers won’t bind to each other.  And then start cycling while we are cooling down, so then we never have a situation where the full reagents are sitting for a long time at a primer-dimer creating temperature.  

I don't think we need to pre-heat the blocks before the cartridge is inserted, but I think that is ok to try. Also we are thinking it is ok if we get early amplification during this cool-down period because amplification that happens at a higher anneal temperature is more likely to be the correct sequence amplification.  This is similar to a PCR method called Touchdown PCR: https://bitesizebio.com/2203/touchdown-pcr-a-primer-and-some-tips/

Culture test result day 6
Good result from the improved parameter on Cap Heater - Double LED setup. Ramp up duration 240s, Bang bang config 5s on, 3s off, no growth both in liquid culture and agar plate. We might want to keep this parameter as the standard for v3.

Even with that result, I still think we can do optimization in the total heating duration but with tweaks in the LED heating parameters.
And finally, no growth observed in all tests with cap heater on, meaning that the cap heater is doing a great job in killing bacteria at least in the bottom surface of the silicon cap.

Seno 

Outline : 
Mechanical testing on the V3 flexure concept (with silicon add-ons) confirms that asymmetric spring preload effectively compensates the gravitational load of the transducer without vertical silicon support. Gap scanning from 10 g up to approximately 2 kg shows no observable separation when preload balance is properly tuned. The horn tip consistently returns to its origin position, indicating stable mechanical equilibrium under varying loads. This configuration is therefore validated as mechanically robust and suitable to proceed as the baseline setup for sonication bench testing.

Sonication Sample Swap (Water to Resuspension) Problem – Reconfirmation and RCA PoC
Reconfirmation of the swapping water-to-resuspension sonication issue shows that the failure mechanism is not transducer degradation but is more like after burn-in effect. Acoustic verification confirms stable resonance behavior, while PoC tests reproduce failure only under misaligned or constrained contact conditions. Passive self-alignment using a silicon O-ring with soft tightening fully restores sonication performance for both water and resuspension samples. However we need comparator data from fresh & newly transducers to confirm the difference between before & after burn-in condition.

“Burn-in reduces non-linear transient behavior and amplitude spikes, making system response more predictable under marginal coupling”

Sonication sample swap sample (water to resus) problem reconfirm and RCA PoC

A .Background

Based on what happen on V2 instrument at a few months ago, we found 

“ after stress test,  transducer (PZTOD35-32SO) could not adapt the soften after-heating vial condition which sign by bad cavitation or need a few cycles attempt first to produce the stable cavitation “ 

That condition give assumption  : 

The transducer getting weak  
The transducer more stable (state as “after burn-in transducer”)
 
For number 1 assumption we have consequences like : 
The transducer getting a highly lifetime degradation
Need to determine the lifecycle level for transducer to put on current instrument to determine sample count capability for handling the risk

For number 2 assumption we have consequences like :
The stable transducer should be handled by a module whose mean form application side we need to determine the more properly and fit to purpose for application which is conveyed by mechanical module. 
Need to state the burn-in method in the bench (the burn-in process stated by experts like Hussein Shekani). 

B. Direction

From both assumptions , for number one assumption I think it seems not to be close to the death transducer because the transducer is still active but it is sensitive to misalignment of horn-vials. If I assume the new transducer has an amplitude spike and that condition could give an additional push when sonicate and compromise the misalignments the assumption number 2 is more close. Because at that time the transducer seems active but it has less amplitude and low anomaly of  spike probability. 

As additional to strengthen the reason, until this time we don't state any burn-in protocol on our sonicator production at bench. So I think the number 2 assumption is closer. Morely, in normal use , we don't find any death transducer even there is transducer which use by Amry to do some bench test which high probability of harmful , and the Transducer doesn't death until now. 

C. PoC of Number 2 Assumption

I did some testing with ultrasonic mic which have a high probability of harmfulness like : 
Sonication on no vial (give Vrms > 1kV)
Sonication on dry vial 
Sonication on horn-vial tilt 

After that test we found that The PZTOD25-47SOB#1 cannot sonicate the resuspension buffer if I put a shim at the back to make tilt condition but in opposite if I use water it could sonicate

The science team has confirmed that last week’s report about a growth in liquid culture in the Double LED - Cap heater config was a false alarm. They stated that the result from the 6th day observation is clear, meaning that there’s no cell growth (it should be amplified in the 6th day, but it’s not).
In fact, all of the tests with Cap heater showed no growth on the agar plate at all. Picture below comparing growth in normal config TS5 (2A & 2B) and Double LED - Cap heater config (6A & 6B).


One result from Single LED - Cap heater config shown cell growth in the liquid, this happened because of an error that I mentioned last week about the incorrect current setting (1A instead of 1.5A for single LED)
All of the tests without the cap heater, both from TS5 and Cap heater off config showed some cell growth in the agar plate.
This is a promising result, but we planned to do a replication test just to confirm these results.
We also noticed the growth in normal config TS5 in recent tests. We have a suspicion that recently the liquid temperature has been reduced due to the switch of the top seal (from latex to silicon cap), we’re currently still investigating this. And if we can prove this, I think we need to consider increasing the bang-bang threshold for all v2 instruments.

Sonicator team  on “Bandwidth” redefinition 
A.Backgorund
The bandwidth word  which we state  like at this comments thread , is no matter on the statement after Amry invented his DIY impedance analyzer by constant current source. The weird thing which we feel after measurement is 
 “ The Impedance measurement always state very narrow bandwidth (couple ten or hertz or even under) but in real physical system The frequency could shift up to 2khz based cavitation existing “ 
The DIY impedance measurement also state the anti resonance which feeling different with Transducer Physical anti resonance
So we think the Sonicator team Core Lead should make a clear on this situation to avoid misunderstanding on both EE & ME sides to avoid misdirection and causing wasted resources.

B.Bandwidth definition from EE and Common sound-acoustical field
In simply way, 

So -3dB as criterion for anti resonance when shift little by little from resonance frequency would note as antiresonance frequency And resulting bandwidth
C.The antiresonance behavior based on mechanical vibration
The condition is like the picture below, so mechanical said on little-off-resonance the displacement Dy start happen but its very small, and still state as proper-longitudinal vibration. So we state before on this Antiresonance frequency minus Resonant frequency as bandwith, Which in the physical field we found when we sweep any frequency around resonance frequency we found an area  of no cavitation in both negative and positive side.  


D. Why the electrical cannot well-detected mechanical bandwidth verison
 Its because the EE bandwidth detect on electrical dB which represent energy from currents amps flow source input which is come from one dimensional displacement from piezo electric


 The EE bandwidth which state by Amry’s DIY impedance analyzer State on only pure longitudinal which should have a quick response because the fully amplitude just only on pure longitudinal and one dimensional displacement which effected converted as electrical db of energy and opposite, when the transducer get transversal is cause the energy which use for Displacement Dy , cannot detected by piezo because the one dimensional typical works of piezo. That's why the energy drop from Amry’s DIY impedance analyzer exceeds 3dB if shift with only a little couple of ten hertz.
 In other words, the tools only detect the very pure longitudinal component of the mechanical vibration mode, a condition that almost never occurs in real transducers because structural stress equilibrium naturally introduces transversal and radial components.
E. The Redefining 
 The mechanical bandwidth re-state as 
“ Mechanical Frequency Cutoff “ 
 Meanwhile the Electrical bandwidth still has the same terms to make EE not confused for doing capacitor and electrical Q research etc.

Outline for Wednesday General Update 
d₃₃ Measurement Tool The d₃₃ tool aims to quantify piezoelectric charge response under known force to trace incoming piezo variability. Machining is complete and the tool is functional despite minor manufacturing defects. We will now collect baseline d₃₃ data to correlate piezo properties with acoustic emission performance.
Ultrasonic Mic Update We have finalized the acoustic data acquisition setup using 192 kHz PreAmps hardware sampling, 384 kHz software upsampling, FFT block 8192, and ASIC block 512 to ensure more accurate resolution & faster response for ultrasonic detection. Our current observations confirm clear differences in acoustic emission between vial–horn gap vs. no-gap conditions and between water vs. resuspension samples. These findings allow us to recap that horn–vial coupling quality strongly influences frequency clarity, cavitation signatures, and spectral distribution. Based on this behavior, we could start developing a Sonication Classification Software . With this zero-version SW classifier, we can start rapid incoming checks and continuous robustness validation across transducer, board, and jig/application concept configurations.

Piezo D33 Checker 

The goal on this tools is to check Incoming Piezo to reveal our piezo variance characteristic which potentially give a variative result to Board-Tranducer tuning and End-performance

Faisal 

d33 Measurement Tools



The determination of d33 piezo, we need a charge amplifier equation:


And this equation, substitute both of equation and we have the d33 formulation:



The procedures for this action are below:
Prepare the sample
Mount the piezo disk/stack between two flat electrodes/clamps. Ensure good electrical contact and uniform mechanical contact.
If using a large external capacitor (for short-circuit condition), connect it across the piezo terminals (parallel) before measurement.
Zero out the charge amplifier (i.e. reset to 0 V output with no load) to nullify offsets.
Apply known force
Using the weight scale for the big force to apply a controlled compressive force (in Newtons) to the piezo. The force can be generated by a press tool or something that generates a big force. Wait until force stabilizes (no drift), ensure no mechanical tilt or misalignment.
Measure the output voltage
Read voltage output VoutV_{out}Vout​ from the charge amplifier (in Volts or mV).
Record the value. If possible, repeat multiple times for accuracy / noise check.
Compute d₃₃ using formula above
Repeat for different force levels
To check linearity, repeat measurement for several force levels (e.g. small → medium → higher force) and compute d₃₃ each time.
Verify that d₃₃ remains roughly constant (linear behavior) across the force range. If results diverge, note non-linearity or possible mechanical issues.

Altius
Cap heater trial
After getting bad results from the pin heater, we moved on with the next plan which is the cap heater.
We set the cap heater temperature to 140°C. This is the optimum temperature to gain quick ramp up, but is still safe for the loadcell (loadcell recommended working temperature range is <40°C).
One problem with the cap heater is that the pressure keeps increasing even when the LED is off. This means that we can’t do the bang bang the same way. 
We then changed the way we control the bang bang. Now, instead of controlling the bang bang with pressure feedback, we control it manually with time delay. Here’s the setup based on our temperature-pressure measurements.

Sample pin heater
We had our initial test with the sample pin heater. Here’s the design link

At first we’re trying to understand if the heat is insulated properly and won’t affect the reading of the loadcell. So, we tried to log the loadcell reading while turning on the heater to 100°C, here’s the data:

From this result, we can see that there’s almost no effect on the loadcell reading, and the insulation seems to be doing a good job.
We then tried to measure the vial air temperature using this configuration (Pin temp set to 100°C). We also turned on the vial LED heater and set the threshold to 140g. Unfortunately, the air temperature only reached 53°C.

We then tried to increase the Pin temperature to 150°C. It looked like we could achieve >80°C of air temperature, but we then realized that we had a problem with the melting 3DP spacer which affected the loadcell reading. We tried to switch the spacer material from PLA to ABS but the issue remained. We also wanted to try using the high temp SLA resin but are still looking for the material in housel.

Looking at the FLIR measurement, the spacer temperature reached 113°C during operation at Pin temp 150°C. The PLA cap seems to be deformed as well.

After this, we tried to measure the vial air temperature, with the vial LED heater off. We tried to eliminate the heating variable from the LED. 
Unfortunately we had bad and weird results, the air temperature could only reach 38°C, and now the loadcell value is drifting as we turn on the pin heater. We’re currently still debugging this issue, we’re afraid that the loadcell might be damaged from overheating. This is the result with Pin temp set at 100°C and 150°C

My short term plan for this week is to release the new consumable (silicone cap, new chip that has shallower/the same as previous with some changes, new sequence that compatible with the silicone cap).
I did some preliminary tests with the chip V2.1.2 & V2.2 (Shallow Channel), V2.1.2 does not need sequence changes, V2.2 requires sequence changes. Before going too far, I just run with injected liquid to see the amplification.
I see if using chip clear was easy to have bubble creation with the speed that we usually use, then I reduce the speed, it helps a lot.
After finding the correct speed, I start doing the thermocycle, it can’t use the same delay, we will usually see this bubble. I have already run it 5x times and it has the same result.

Temporary summary:
Shallow channel having 2x slug length which has a very high risk of hitting the branch during primer mixing when having a preheat & and 15x mixing like previous sequence, because in this step the slug adjuster is not adjusting the slug & the slug condition is shifting too much especially if injected.
Injecting the liquid to the chip is harder now, because the depth is too short, our tip can’t enter fully and dispense the liquid properly.
Shallow channel is promising to cut the pcr time consuming, from 35mins to 20 mins
I think using this shallow channel is promising by changing the sequence a lot to make the movement more safe
We need to do DOE once we get the liquid movement sequence since using the current PCR parameter we will see a bubble, I think because our length is increased and the thickness is thinner make the tension between each liquid weaker now.
On V2.2 having lower amplification peak compared to V2.0, it is almost 2x times, I know because it can’t use the same delay as on V2.0.

We've found strange condition here when we see that the culture result show the config II & III grow the MSM bacteria when it supposed to neutralize them, because this config reach higher air temperature. The spread culture of MSM also show similar result when the bacteria colony growth

The most strange condition is when we saw that config V & VII (normal current device setup, threshold 140) also grow the bacteria both in the vial culture and spread, which is make us wonder what's wrong here.
 

So currently we decide to clarify the current device setup and the testing result show the liquid temperature reach desired condition to kill bacteria, and surprisingly the liquid temperature on our 1st  run doesn't spent most of the time beyond 95º, instead they go below it. The 2nd run become worst they only reach 91ºC and spent most of the time below 90º although they reach desired threshold. (H7 & H8) 
Then air temperature measurement of config II & III show 110 max temperature a.k.a the boiling point of the liquid, which is supposed to kill the bacteria (H9)
This growth might be caused by extended observation period happened, because usually we observe the sample in three days and for this one we run it on wednesday last week and observe it this monday so 5 day. So we planned to conduct 2nd bacteria culture to clarify our previous result tomorrow, and see the result exactly three days later.

Device Testing
Preheat test
After we found that a temperature of 100°C gave good results, in this test we tried gradually reducing the gblocks from 10K to 100 copies. The results using a preheat temperature of 100°C can detect up to 100 copies of gblocks.
Rif assay test
From the previous test FS2/RS2 showed good results, so in this test we want to see when using lower copies of gblocks. We used from 66k to 6.6 copies of WT gblocks and the results showed that FS2/RS2 with LNA P3 can detect up to 66 copies, meanwhile using 6.6 copies showed flat in all runs.

Asymmetric primers
We tried to replicate an asymmetric primer test based on FUSA’s test using FS2/R primer and Taqman probe (WT450 ROX) with WT450 and SNP450 targets (10^5 cp/ul). In the first trial, we failed to show good results because the melt curve setup for the ROX filter was unchecked. The second trial, the melt curve appeared but they did not seem as smooth as FUSA’s results. And according to Sean’s comments, we just knew that the setup for the melt curve itself needed a bit of adjustment. We then tried it again, and succeeded in demonstrating melt curve analysis using asymmetric primer in the third trial. 

Combination of Evagreen for Melt Curve in FAM and Asymmetric Primer for Melt Curve in ROX
For the purpose of analyzing the co-reaction between EvaGreen and the asymmetric primer, we conducted a test using the setups listed below:
IS6110 (FAM) + WT-450 asymmetric (ROX) + EvaGreen
IS6110 (FAM) + EvaGreen
WT-450 asymmetric (ROX)
IS6110 (FAM) + WT-450 + EvaGreen
In this experiment, the results indicated that the combination of EvaGreen and the asymmetric primer did not produce any negative effects on the melt curve. Instead, this combination increased the derivative reporter signal.

Rif LNA-P450 Assays Testing
There are several tests that we did to choose the selected primer for LNA-P450m. As we mentioned below:
Primer Selection ()
Tongue Swab Reactivity ()
Primer Asymmetry and 4x Probe’s concentration ()
	In the first test, we evaluated the quality of each primer and identified FS2/R and FS1/RP as the most robust and compatible primer sets for the LNA-P450m target (Figure 1). In the second test, the selected primers were evaluated under tongue swab conditions. Among the four primer combinations tested, FS2/R and F1/RP showed the least interference from the tongue swab condition and exhibited low primer–dimer formation (Figure 2&3). The final test was conducted to determine the effect of asymmetric primers (1 forward primer to 10 reverse primers) and an increased probe concentration (4× higher than the normal concentration) on PCR activity. In this test, we found that neither asymmetric primers nor a higher probe concentration produced a significant improvement in PCR performance (Figure 4).

HS fTaq Tests for QS6
Previously in DE, there was several tests to create a HS fTaq with this antibody from DSBio. There was no significant improvement in the HS version and the tests was not resumed. A quick look at the older journals shows that the fTaq was not mixed 1:1: with the antitaq, which could’ve resulted in just some of the fTaq in the mix to become HS, with most of it being just plain old taq with the same performance. 
Now there is more interest in testing out a HS version of fTaq, and here I tested it out with and without TS samples to see if this helps in overcoming the inhibitory effect of TS samples, and increasing sensitivity by reducing non specific amplicons. The HS fTaq performed much better than the regular version, however, the amount of  amplicons on the lower Tm side remained somewhat significant when compared to the non HS version. It is also interesting to note that all of our melt curves contain much more background compared to tests done during DE days (comparison only relevant to no TS rxn). 
I only have less than 10uL of the antibody left, but have ordered more that should arrive by the end of March. For now, all of HS fTaq tests can only be done in liquid form as the antibody itself contains glycerol. If we want more for dried chips, dialysing the glycerol out with pulse could be an option.

Contamination Issue

4.  fTaq vs Meridian with IS assay.
Here I compared the performance of fTaq vs Meridian with the IS 6 and 10 assays, with and without human assay and TS. And it is pretty clear that the weak IS assay performance that have been observed is worse with fTaq compared to meridian.  Note : the meridian mastermix (polymerase +assay+gblock) was prepared the day before the test ( leftovers from this experiment) and was stored in 4C overnight. 
5. Preliminary test with Proteinase K
I have been considering trying out PK as a way to reduce the strong inhibitory effect that we observe with saliva for some time, especially with fTaq. PK has been reported in multiple studies to help with sensitivity in PCR with saliva (mostly with RNA extraction for covid detection). There was a concern on if the PK would inhibit the fTaq if we don’t wash it away / remove it from the mixture, but a heat precipitation step was thought to be enough to remove any negative effects. However from these results, the addition of PK has slightly worse results when compared to no PK.  
The stock of PK that we have is from 2023, and has been stored in our -20C freezer all this time.
0.25 ug of PK (following this paper)  was added to the TS and incubated at 58C for 1 hr. We could change the amount and time used if necessary.

February 10 - 16,  2026
Device Testing
Assay Compatibility Testing
We checked the compatibility of LNA P 3 ROX with KAT G and FAB G using DsBio in all reactions and we did it with injection set up. The results show that KAT G and FAB G have good performance. The graph is high, meanwhile the LNA P 3 has lower amplification not as high as KAT G and FAB G.

Preliminary Test
We also did a preliminary test in devices 003, 005, and 006 before going into the BSL testing. We did it with dry chips and we also added 1M copies/600 uL of IS6110, WT and Human gBlock on the resuspension buffer. The results showed that all the channels have amplification without the tongue swab samples and on the FAM channel the stdev value shows a number below 1, except on the ROX channel.

Preliminary test in BSl-2 inhouse
The previous reproducibility of 4 devices (without TS sample) were good by its stdev less than 1. Otherwise, inconsistent results were observed across the devices when TS sample was introduced (esp on low copies gblock, its start to on off at 10K cp/gblock). We suspected whether it's due to the inhibitory sample itself or the sonicator process decreasing low copies gblock performance. We want to test low copies of gblock on devices just to see their performance without TS sample. since the previous reproducibility results used high copies gblock

To find out the sonication effect on low copies of gblock, we did test using QS6 using several concentrations of IS6110 gblock (100k - 100 cp) without TS sample. Then the results, the effect of sonication on high copies gblock showed significant results on the cqs and the curve amplitude, while in lower gblocks (1000 - 100) only effecting on the curves amplitudes. These results werent as our assumption because we think that after the sonication process, the gblock will decrease in performance. One of our suspects was, we mixed the fTaq at the very first with the samples then kept them in the fridge before the PCR process. Maybe good results came from non specific amplicons. and the other suspect maybe, sonication process mixed the gblock very well eventho we did manual tip mix to aliquot the sample as before sonication.
We then conducted the 2nd exp exactly the same as the 1st exp to validate the results. In this test we added fTaq right after all the samples were ready to be run in QS6. Therefore, the same result pattern was observed as the previous test. as the conclusions, there is no impact before and after sonication on low cp gblock. The prelim results in inhouse BSL with 4 devices were bad on low cp gblock was purely because of the introducing of TS sample as the inhibitory things


Dry Chip Development

Selection of the rifampicin assay
Based on the results and discussions with the team, we will use only one rifampicin assay so that Ftaq can still be tested on the same chip with DS Bio HS at RSPAW. We selected between the LNAP-3 FS1/RS1 and LNAP-3 FS1/RS1a assays, as both showed better performance compared to the other LNAP-3 assays.
Our selection was based on run results from batch 665, which was tested with TS samples, as well as batches 660, 661, and 662, which were run without TS samples. The results showed that LNAP-3 FS1/RS1 was relatively more consistent than LNAP 3 RS1 Fs1 a. Therefore, we selected the LNAP 3 RS1 Fs1 assay to be used on the chip that will be tested at RSPAW.
QC Batch 666
The curve quality is quite good. The IS double assay has an A value of around 250, while LNA Fab G and Human are around 100. Although general cleaning has been performed, human DNA contamination is still present. We plan to replace several reagents suspected of contamination. result
QC Batch 667
The curve quality is quite good. DS Bio IS6110 and IS1081, DS Bio Rif FS1 RS1, DS Bio Kat G, and Ftaq FS1 RS1 have A values of 200–250. DS Bio HS Human, as usual with this sequence, has an A value below 100. Unfortunately, Ftaq Kat G has an A value below 200. We also still detected human DNA contamination and slight WT contamination in Kat G, even though general cleaning has been performed. We suspect there is contamination in the Kat G assay. We will try replacing it with a new stock. result

February 3 - 9,  2026
Device Testing
Reproducibilty
We check the reproducibility of the new sequence in all V2 devices. We run it with injection, vial, and full sequence.
Run #1 - #8 used injected sequences (IS6110 6600 copies and Human 6600 copies).
Run #9 - #12 used vial sequence with dried chip without heating and sonicating (IS6110 1M copies, MSM 1M copies and Human 1M cp).
Run #13 - #20 used full sequence with heating and sonicating
		The results : 
- Run with Injected sequence show standard deviation (stdev) below than 1 on most channel except channel 4, because we got weak amplification at Run#2
-  Run with vial and full sequence shows all channels have stdev below than 1.
-  We got some aborted in full sequence because the latex is leaking, so we must rework the latex first before running the process.

Reproducibility with gBlock
The next day we continued the reproducibility test using device 003, 005, 006. In this test we used the gBlock of MSM, IS6110 , and Human with 1M copies and we used the full sequence set up. 
We still got several aborted runs because the latex leaked even after we retouch and used a latex cover. So that’s why in several runs we replaced the latex with a new one and successfully completed the run.

Reproducibility with MSM bacteria
The next day we continued the reproducibility test, in this test we used the MSM bacteria with 100k CFU/600uL and 1M copies/600 uL of IS6110 and Human gBlock. The results when using the MSM bacteria the stdev can below than 1 between run and devices, and also in device 3 at channel 3 the ct shows around 17 in most of run it’s different from other devices that makes higher stdev than other channels.


Dry Chip Development
Carried out production of Dry Chips for batches 662 and 663 and performed quality control. Both batches showed good quality curves; however, there was amplicon contamination. Therefore, we conducted a general cleaning of the production laboratory on February 5, 2026.

Plan
Test the stability of the Dry Chip at 80°C for 30 minutes after 1 month of storage. The test will be conducted on February 13, 2026.
If the chips remain stable after one month, we will begin preparing 36 chips for a one-year stability study. We will test 3 chips per month.
Test the tolerance limits of the baking temperature. We will test 5°C below and 5°C above the set point, so the temperatures to be tested are 75°C, 80°C, and 85°C.
Prepare Dry Chips for in-house testing.
Prepare Dry Chips for testing with RSPAW and R2D2 samples.


DSBio LOD with TS Previous results showed that DSBio could detect low copies of gblock (up to 5 cp/uL), yet the negative control also showed amplification. We then retested it again after making new reagent stocks and the results showed that we still had persistent amplicons (the amplicons produced having the same or similar peak with the target (IS6110) by the melt curves) and the same pattern was observed when the addition of TS had inhibitory effects towards DSB performance as previous results. Because of those persistent amplicons, we did general lab cleaning the next day.

January 26, 2026 - 
Assay concentrations affecting tongue swab sample curves (FUSA)
Increasing assay concentration in the reaction increased signal to background for control and tongue swab reactions. Just an example of another basic reagent component that we could adjust to potentially improve tongue swab PCR reactions, especially at low target concentrations. (link)

Testing PCR enhancers with tongue swab samples (FUSA)
Performed a couple of quick experiments with PEG or carnitine added to the PCR reaction. No clear improvement in this first experiment, but just an example of additives we could try later. (link). Previous carnitine result from the DE project with saliva (link).
 

January 20, 2026 - MgCl2 concentrations affecting tongue swab sample curves (FUSA)
As I mentioned in the January 19 Scientist Meeting below, I have done some testing in FUSA in which I have evaluated different concentrations of MgCl2 (1.25 mM to 3.75 mM) in the PCR reaction with low copy target (100 cp/rxn) tongue swab and control samples. At mid and higher level MgCl2 concentrations, Taqman curves were more robust with early Cts, but high concentrations did result in higher ratios of non-specific primer dimer amplification. See Experiments 3 through 5 (link). It is worth evaluating MgCl2 concentrations with our tongue swab device and low target concentration. DSBio example from H2 2025 (link).

January 12-19,  2026
Device Testing
Preliminary test, MSM with HS DSBio
Since we did not have data of MSM cells using HS DsBio, we tried to collect this data using liquid set up.
We spike 30uL to swab stick from concentrations : 
5 M CFU / mL -> final concentration 6750 cfu / 40 uL.
2.5 M CFU / mL -> final concentration 3375 cfu / 40uL.
1.25 M CFU / mL -> final concentration 1687 cfu / 40uL.
The mechanism is we heat and sonicate the sample in the first, then we take 27uL of sample and add with 13uL of formulation of assay and polymerase, after that the solution is injected into the channel and run in the 005 device.


BSL-2 inhouse testingThe first objective is to test MSM cells that spiked to TS and sputum samples. In this test, 20 uL from 5 M CFU/mL MSM cells was spiked onto tongue swab (100K CFU on TS sample). Afterwards, the tongue swab is going through a full process in device 005 with 600 uL resuspension buffer. And then, est final concentration is around 40/600*100,000 = 6,666 CFU/channel. On the other hand for sputum test, 100 uL from MSM stock (16 M CFU/mL) was added to sputum 17 (saliva) which est vol around 500 uL. est final concentration of MSM after blank flock swab dipped to saliva is around 80K - 100K CFU/swab. What we observed from this test, so far: HS DSBio perform better than the ftaq to detect MSM and the curves were sigmoid while using blank flock swab (ctrl), but HS DSBio curves start sloping when we introduce TS and sputum sample. Another thing is, it is nice to see that the fTaq could detect MSM cells, because from the previous data, when we try it with liquid set up with MSM cell, ftaq couldn't detect it (the curve was almost flat, data: 12/03/2025; 12/05/2025; 12/11/2025)

The second objective is to test IS6110 gBlock 100K copies/600 uL that spiked into vial tube. Run #1 and #2, run as a control without TS or sputum, all channel were amplified on DSbio and Ftaq. Run #3 and #4, added TS sample to vial, the result shows on Ftaq has weak amplification while DSbio shows strong curve. Run #5 and 6 (contains TS samples that was dipped to sputum that was spiked with gblock) were aborted early in the cycling process. The sample used in run#5 was visibly more viscous compared to normal TS samples, which could've caused the run to be aborted since it hindered the liquid movement. In run#7 and *8 (blank floqswab dipped into saliva-purulent sputum, fTaq did not exhibit any activity and DSBio produced curve with poor quality for the IS6110 assay. Both polymerase performed well for the human assay. In run#9 and #10 (blank floqswab dipped into saliva), both polymerase performed well and matched it's control performance

Figure 1. Trend of a-value

The drying parameter 50C 80 min shows good stability in two weeks. The CT and b-values showed low standard deviation across both IS6110-FAM and Human-ROX channels, indicating good stability after drying (Table 1). In contrast, the a-value showed higher variability, especially at early time points. However, as shown in Table 2 and Figure 1, the average a-value decreased sharply from day 0 to day 6, then became relatively stable after that for both DSBio HS and fTaq. Although some fluctuation was still observed, the overall trend suggests that the dry chip maintained acceptable performance for up to two weeks. 

80C 30 mins
Since the previous experiment showed no significant difference in performance between chips baked at 50°C for 80 minutes and those baked at 80°C for 30 minutes, we repeated the stability test at 80°C for 30 minutes. New testing was conducted on day 0 and day 1 and will be continued for up to two weeks. The 50°C for 80 minutes parameter is still feasible for producing 36 chips per day; however, the 80°C for 30 minutes condition provides greater time flexibility, especially  for Quality Control or to enable a further increase in production quantity.

Figure 1. Trend of a-value

	
	

Ct- and b-values were within the acceptable range (Tables 1 and 3). However, the a-value from the new stability test at 50°C for 80 minutes still needs further observation, because only one replicate is available after Bayu’s repair (day 6). As shown in Figure 1, the a-value for the 50°C, 80-minute condition on day 6 was much lower than on day 0, which caused a high standard deviation. In contrast, the standard deviation among replicates on the same day was low, showing good repeatability (Table 2). A similar result was also seen in the first test of the 80 °C, 30-minute condition, where the standard deviation on the same day was generally small (Table 4). The 80°C, 30-minute condition will be tested again after we prepare it using the repaired sealer machine, and the stability test will be continued for at least two more weeks.

Scale-up of dry chip production
We evaluated the impact of scaling up production from 18 to 36 chips. Chip numbers 1, 2, 17, 18, 19, 20, 34, and 35 were sampled, while chip number 36 was used as a negative control. January 6, 2026 - Using anneal/extend temp to improve sensitivity
In the FUSA meeting today, we discussed a quick test that could be done to potentially reduce non-specific amplification and increase sensitivity with low target concentrations. We recommend testing increasing the anneal/extend temperature to reduce non-specific amplification (could be relevant to both fTaq and DSBio reactions). Below is an example gradient PCR experiment that I performed last month with fTaq and 100 copies of IS6110 gBlock and Evagreen. In this experiment (69C-circles and 65C-crosses) yielded more robust and target-specific curves than 61C anneal/extend. It is definitely worth revisiting increased anneal/extend temps across a concentration range of target on the tongue swab device. Since previous optimization has only been performed at high target concentration, the impact on specificity and sensitivity was likely masked.

December 31 - January 5,  2025 - 2026

Demonstrating the specificity risk of allowing an fTaq reaction that was preheated to 85C and allowed to dwell at 85C before PCR cycling. (FUSA Testing).
Preheating the reaction to 85C prior to fTaq addition, then allowing the reaction to remain at 85C for 3 min (the time needed on the current tongue swab device to cool from 85C to 60C) results in high levels of non-specific primer-dimer amplification and no target-specific amplification -link.
However, preheating the reaction to 95C prior to fTaq addition, then allowing the reaction to remain at 95C for 3 min resulted in robust Taqman curves and target-specific amplicon melt curves - link.

Using touchdown PCR to improve fTaq reaction specificity and work with a condition in which the cold block on the tongue swab can cool from denature temp to anneal/extend temp during PCR. (FUSA Testing).
Preheating the reaction to 95C prior to fTaq addition, then proceeding directly to PCR in which the anneal/extend temp decreases from 95C to 65C during cycling resulted in robust target-specific amplicon curves and melt curves and very late PCR non-specific curves for the NTC - link.

Dry Chip Sensitivity
In the previous experiment, using Ftaq batch D0411 at a 1:5 dilution, we increased the Ftaq concentration (plus trehalose and BSA) by 20% and 40% from the normal formulation, which improved Ftaq sensitivity at 66 copies/channel.
We then repeated the experiment using batch D1212 at a 1:40 dilution (since the Ftaq concentration in this batch is higher than that of batch D0411); however, the results appeared inconsistent.
Before proceeding further with experiments to improve Ftaq sensitivity, in my opinion it would be better to develop a quantitative or semi-quantitative polymerase calculation method. This would facilitate future enzyme development, both in liquid and dry formats. If the enzyme source has a consistent concentration, the results of other research activities will also be more consistent. Ode has experience in semi-quantitative protein quantification from his research prior to joining Formulatrix, and he may be able to try applying it again (just a small suggestion from my side).
c) Scale-up of dry chip production
On January 8, 2026, we will evaluate a production scenario from Munis by increasing output from 18 chips/day to 36 chips/day, and assess it using a systematic interval sampling method to determine whether this scale-up impacts dry chip quality.

December 24-30,  2025 
Demonstrating the specificity risk of allowing an fTaq reaction that was preheated to 85C to slowly cool to 60C before PCR cycling. (FUSA Testing).
Preheating the reaction to 85C prior to fTaq addition, then allowing the reaction to slowly cool to 60C (similar to current tongue swab device preheat sequence) results in high levels of non-specific primer-dimer amplification and no target-specific amplification -l ink. On the tongue swab device, the preheated fTaq reaction should not be allowed to cool below 85C prior to PCR cycling. 

Preliminary check on preheating to 85C during mixing. 
Previously, we have started to use 65C during preheating. When the temperature was raised to 85C and tested on dried chips, it flatlined. The chips from the same batch performed fine when the preheating temperature was set to 65C. Link to journal entry here .  


Preheating set at 65 C
	
	
Preheating set at 85 C
	
	


Since this was done with dried chips, there was a slight doubt on whether the dried assay in the channel is somehow affected negatively when the heatpad is ramping up its temperature. The dried assay is not directly on top of the heatpad, but 85C is relatively high and the dried assay is not too far from the heatpad. To proof this, the runs were repeated with liquid reactions with the preheating set to 65C and 85C. Turns out, a similar pattern was observed, where the reactions that had a higher preheating temperature performed very poorly.  Ignore the difference in reactions per chip for the dried test shown above to the liquid test shown below. The dried test used leftover chips that were made for dilution tests for a previous batch. 

Preheating set at 65 C
	
	
Preheating set at 85 C
	
	


This leads me to believe that the higher temperature somehow negatively affects the components in the mixture.This results are also different from what was observed in FUSA, where preheating (up to 90C) improves specificity (judged by meltcurves). It is worth noting that there are a few differences between FUSA’s experiments and ours : 
fTaq is added after the preheating in FUSA. Here, the mix already contains all components since we cannot add anything after the chip is sealed.
FUSA uses a qPCR machine instead of TS device. 
Most of the data uses commercial Taq. 

Technically, all the components that we use (probes and primers, buffer, and  dNTPs, even the fTaq) are thermostable at 85C, or even higher. I am not sure how the combination of using our TS device (instead of a regular qPCR machine) and preheating for a brief moment causes the performance to become significantly worse. More tests needs to be done to understand what we can improve if we want to incorporate a preheating step. 

Is it the fTaq that is negatively affected by the heating that causes weak activity? Unlikely since taq is arguably more thermostable compared to all the other components. 
Other components somehow cannot handle the heat? If so, why? They’re all fine at 95C during the denaturation step. The preheating step does not take too long either. 
Is the liquid heating up too rapidly in the channel during the mixing step?
If the issue is heat-caused damage, it’s worth trying to add more sugar based enhancers. Most of them are known to act as stabilizers and thermoprotectants. We already use trehalose. Sucrose is another option.

Adding Evagreen to TS tests.
Link to report 
The initial idea to add evagreen is to gauge the amount of nonspecific amplicons that’s produced when reactions are run in the TS device, especially when we use low copy gblocks. I ran some tests with and without fluorescence, with and without evagreen to see how it affected the baseline. 

In the buffer only runs, addition of evagreen did not increase the baseline significantly. However, when it is added to reactions that contains assays, the baseline increases. And the curve amplitude increases significantly in just the first few cycles of the log phase. This could indicate that there’s a lot of non-specific amplification happening and the evagreen is binding to all the double stranded nucleotides. It is also important to note that the oversaturation is caused by the relatively low limit of our lenses. It would be interesting to see if other polymerase other than fTaq (especially hotstart ones) has the same reactions

Primary Test Ftaq Batch D1212
Batch D1212 shows different quality compared to D0411; at the same dilution, the results are significantly different. This is likely due to the Ftaq source being too concentrated, based on the results of the Kukuh and Ode tests.

DMSO test on QS6 Machine for DSBio Regular and fTaq
Based on our experiment, addition of DMSO reduces the amplification curve quality, both for DSBio Regular and fTaq. It also reduces the melt curves.


	
	

Dilution test fTaq batch d1212 (in QS6)
We did a dilution test from undiluted to 1:60. fTaq d1212 has the best performance when it is diluted to 1:50. It is different compared to last fTaq’s batch when the best performance in liquid form is on dilution 1:10-1:20. We assume the differences from its results because the concentration of this fTaq production batch is higher than previous batches. First experiment, second experiment.

Dried fTaq batch d1212 Performance in TS device. 
This experiment tested dried fTaq on the TS device, with the fTaq diluted from 1/20x to 1/50x. The previous batch of fTaq batch d0411 (diluted 1/5x) was used as a control to evaluate the differences between the two batches. From the runs, it shows that batch d1212 has the best performance when diluted to 1/30 - 1/40x, and had comparable performance when diluted even lower to a 1/5x fTaq d0411. In higher concentrations, it has poor performance. 

Other than different concentrations (final yield) between the two batches and the source culture used, there is no significant difference in the process of purifying these two batches of fTaq. But from the preliminary results, the two batches are observed to have different performances, with the d1212 being the better out of the two. 



Cross Contamination Test
A preliminary test for BSL 2 testing using IS6110/IS1081, RIF and INH assays with several gblocks, in order to get cross reaction data when we use full process setup within the device. 

The result of this experiment is that the KatG and FabG targets were not amplified, which was attributed to the use of incorrectly selected gBlocks. Based on our prior data, WT gBlocks should be used rather than KatG and FabG gBlocks. 

Hereafter, IS6110 and IS1081 gBlocks were unexpectedly amplified by the RpoB (RIF) assay, despite not being the intended targets. This was evidenced by amplification observed in Test 1 and Test 2 when IS6110/IS1081 gBlocks were tested with the FS1/RS1 primer set, which is specific for the RIF assay. In addition, weak late-cycle amplification (approximately cycle 40) was detected in the IS6110/IS1081 channels in both tests. Here

In the liquid culture, only config 8 showed clear results after 3 and 6 days observation.
