Device Testing
Since we don't have any data of ftaq with MSM cells, we start to do baselining with device 005. The idea of this test is by spiking the MSM cells to the flock swab then put it into the vials containing M1N(2) buffer and do heating and sonication within the device. After that, the lysate is injected to the channel for further PCR process. 
The first experiment, with 6750 cfu/rxn final concentration of MSM cells in each channel, DSBio with Device Testing - H1 2026M1N2 buffer still becomes the best polymerase with robust amplification curve. Meanwhile, fTaq still did not give promising data with current formulation. We also did a reality check on QS6, just to check if the ftaq was still performing good or not by comparing it with other polymerases. Based on the QS6 results, all of the polymerase exhibits an amplification, with the most robust curve was fTaq 1/5 dilution.
Knowing that the ftaq was fine, we then assumed that maybe ftaq was less sensitive in the device compared to other polymerases. To prove that, the second experiment was conducted by doubling the MSM cells that were spiked on the flock swab. The results shows ftaq was pretty good at detecting higher concentration of MSM cells but still the curved amplitude wasn't that robust as DSbio in detecting 6750 cfu/ rxn. one thing that we want to improve for ftaq sensitivity is by using DMSO, because janavi's test with DMSO could improve ftaq performance in the device.

LOD testing using DSbio. At first LOD we only used IS6110 assay and got amplified of NC. On the other day we used IS6110, IS1081 and a combination of both of them. When testing enhancers with low copy templates (for sensitivity), I have started to include melt curve analysis as well (sensitivity). The melt curve uses ftaq with higher copy number template (10e5) and meridian as a control. So far I have tried testing with 3 enhancers, including continuing DMSO from the previous weeks.

LED heater validation (device 006)
As we knew that the engineer was still looking for the optimum parameter for the LED heater (the temp inside the vial, both liquid and the air must be as high as possible) for inactivation of the MTB cell without damaging the DNA. so, we did V2 device validation (006) by seeing the MSM cells amplification and the culture (here) with several parameters.


Config V and VII are our current parameter (140 th, 5 mins beng beng) as the control, the cq mean in config V (sonicated after heating) shows the best result among the other config, but it's also shown that the MSM cells were still growing after 5 days of incubation.
Config II and III  had the same set up, in which there was no sonicator contact and vial opener during the heating process for 10 mins. config III (sonicated after heating) gave the second best results after config V in cq means, but then the MSM cells were still growing after 5 days of incubation.
Config VIII and IX are using double LEDs for heating with the highest threshold. There were 3 cq gaps in the cqs (config IX, sonicated after heating) compared to config V. However, the MSM cells were not growing after 5 days of incubation. This config was good to inactivate bacteria, but on the other hand it can damage the DNA. 

In addition to liquid culture, we did the swab test that was taken from water vapour on the top  silicon cap after the heating process. In short, the steps to do that are as follows:  before the heating process, we inverted the vial upside down for 5x to make the MSM dispersed all around the vials, then exposed to the heater (as the config set up), and last we took the water vapour by swabbing it (with onemed swab) and did a streak on MB agar plate. Among several configs, only config IX (double LEDs) shows no MSM cells growth after 5 days incubation. 



Previous test shows that config III and V had almost similar cq mean, so we did follow-up validation test of the V2 device by only monitoring MSM amplification to gather more data on that configuration (here).

Previous config III is the same as config IV, while previous config V is the same as config II in this test. As we can see, they still gave different results (the cq means between them is less than 1). But again, both configs couldn't inactivate the MSM cell based on the culture on previous data.

fTaq Sensitivity Improvement for Low Copy templates w/ DMSO
When using DMSO for almost all of my previous experiments, the amount used was always 2% for all template concentration. This amount was based on this testing, where the addition 2% DMSO had the most promising result when compared to 1-3%. However, I feel like that should have been explored more as the addition of 2% DMSO in  the upcoming tests did not help much with lower copies (660 and below). 

Another test was done with gradient levels of DMSO ranging from 2, 4 and 6% with 660 copies of templates (since this seems to be the cutoff for sensitivity). The experiment was repeated with annealing set at 55C and 57.5C. It appears that 4% DMSO increases PCR performance better as opposed to the original 2%, with the annealing set at 57.5 C for 660 copies of template. Between the two chosen annealing temperature, it is not quite clear which one is better, or if I can still tinker around with it (increasing it by one degree perhaps). The use of 57.5C is slightly better with the current amount of tests done when compared to 55C, but it has greater run-to-run variability. Here’s a comparison between the 2 temperatures with varying amount of DMSO with 660 copies of template (IS6110 FAM channel only). 
When the same set-up is tried for even lower copies (66 copies), similar enhancement was not observed. This can be explained by the fact that out fTaq has reached its limit, or there are some more alterations that can be done. The curve quality that is observed with 66copies is mostly flat, with some curves. Link to report : 

Prior to this, I tried to combine adding  2%DMSO and additional fTaq to see if that would increase performance, since the 2%DMSO only would not help much when we are processing TS samples. The results did not show any significant improvement. Link to report : . 
I also did baseline testing for fTaq + DMSO on QS6, it  can be found here   

For the following week, I will be focusing on trying to reproduce the results with dried fTaq using formula 2, where I will do a LoD test for dried fTaq, and then attempt to see if DMSO in the resuspension buffer could increase sensitivity as it did with the liquid tests. 


Dry Chip Development 
Rif assay
In batch 630, channel 1 containing Meridian RIF appeared not sufficiently dried. I confirmed this with Adit and Bowo, and Bowo also observed that the same channel was not fully dry in several chips. For that chip, I only added the assay and Meridian. Normally, when using a single-plex with Meridian, I include NFW, but in that experiment I did not. I then repeated the process in batch 631. After adding NFW,the dried looks dry and the amplification curves look good. All three RIF variants I tested (Meridian Fs1/Rs1-FAM, Meridian Fs1/Rs1a-FAM, Meridian Fs1/Rs2a-FAM) showed good amplification.  In channel 4, the Meridian IS6110-FAM curve appears shorter in quality, and the slug also looks shorter than the others, even though its liquid volume was the same as the rest.I still don’t know the cause.




Sensitivity improvement
I understand that increasing the amount of enzyme means increasing the amount of preservative reagent as well. However, in this experiment, I only increased the amount of enzyme to determine the limit of the preservative reagent’s ability to maintain the enzyme. In this experiment, we can see that adding 20% more enzyme without increasing the preservative reagent improves the curve quality. However, after increasing the enzyme by 40% and then 60%, the curve quality drops significantly. This occurs because the amount of preservative reagent is no longer sufficient.  

If we want to increase the amount of polymerase, we also need to increase the preservative reagents, The preservative reagent must be kept at the concentration :  trehalose 1 M at 9.5% of the total dried volume, and BSA 10 mg/mL at 10.8% of the total dried volume. In the next experiment, I will increase the concentration of the preservative reagent in FTAq by adding 40% and 60% concentration increments.and then test it at a lower gBlock concentration.

Hi Kyle,
 
For long duration shipments (7-10days) we need to use packaging large enough to accommodate the amount of dry-ice needed. Unless the package will be re-filled with dry-ice while in transit, 70-80lbs of dry-ice will be needed for a 6-day where the dry-ice will need to be refilled before the 2nd transport leg. This is also assuming there will be no delay’s with customs clearance which will add more time to the shipment transit duration. It is recommended to re-plenish dry-ice every 3 days in transit.
 
Is it possible to contact the courier to discuss:
Packaging availability to transport samples +110lbs of dry-ice
Is the courier able to provide the service to re-plenish dry-ice in transit every 3 days?
Can the courier ensure quick customs clearance to avoid delays?
 
Please let me know if you need any other information!
Thank you!
 
Regards,
Kevin

CPB (GMP for Medical Devices)
The Ministry of Health of the Republic of Indonesia has implemented a new regulation regarding the certification process, where CPAKB has changed its name to CPB (Cara Pembuatan yang Baik)
Summary
About the Technical Responsibility Person (PJT). All classification details before PERMENKES-11-2025.pdf are No longer in effect / No longer valid. So the technical Responsibility Person(PJT) classification just to be simple as just having a minimum education degree; "A Diploma degree (D3) in technical, science, or medical fields." but must still have certification for Technical responsibility person CPB.
There is a new update regarding the certification application process. Previously, there were no limits on additional data submissions. However, a limitation has now been implemented: a maximum of 2 submissions during the document review process and 2 submissions for CAPA/CAR fulfillment.

Ibeng Bandung visit
In Thursday morning I visited Prof Bhacti company. (Pakar Biomedika) and meet their RnD representatives Anita.

Small company of 20-ish people that produces rapid test kit for many diseasses and narcotics.
Have ISO 13485, CPAKB, CPAKD, and some of their product TKDN certified.
Facilities isn’t that impressive, lab is not particularly clean room either, but well organized
But their production use humidity controlled room that keep the humidity level below 30% to keep the quality of their dryed reagent
Their RnD is only on formulation, others part is mostly imported.
I asked her with this much variety of products, how hard is it to acquire izin edar. She said it’s quite simple, just product specification, clinical trial document, SOP, etc. But there are no minimum sensitivity or specivity.
She said that TKDN is not simple and there are indication of collusion because even when their company develop and manufacture the products locally, their TKDN percentage is not as high as their hope. But other company that only do repackaging of imported products can get higher TKDN.
When visiting their lab, there is electrical blackout for quite long time, and their refrigerator with a lot of expensive chemicals also turn off but they don’t seem worried at all. I just find that funny.
After that, around 11am I go to Pusat Akademik, Inovasi, Teknologi, dan Riset Kesehatan Universitas Padjadjaran (Centre of Academics, Innovation, Technology, and Health Research of Unpad). Just across the street from Labkesda Jabar (one of lab for izin edar verification) and Hasan Sadikin Hospital (Main hospital at West Java) to meet Prof Bhacti and his team. Also it’s near FORB, only around 4km.

It’s a research centre that also have clinic that specializes for TB and HIV patient. So they always have samples ready.
Their TB work is mostly funded by Gates foundation
They are testing the devices that are sponsored by Gates foundation so it can be adopted by government ASAP. Pluslife is their first success, but I see they also test other devices, currently testing portnat (link) and I see one box from other manufacturer waiting in queue for testing.
Discussion with Prof Bhacti and his team

They are very interested in our company, especially after I showed them our line of products. They keep asking if we can make costum machine.
The TB diagnostic test market is surely crowded right now. We need an angle to stand out from the rest. He said the TB test plus rif resistance testing with low price sure can be an advantage.
Prof Bhacti is the one that write testing standard regulation for izin edar. Pluslife got izin edar before he write the rules. He said sorry but it’s better than try to get WHO PQ.
It looks like MoH really listen to their opinion and recommendation. Many of their study is to shape the TB policy nationally.
Even when I’m there, I can’t meet with Bonny beacuse she had a meeting with MoH peoples. Asking for her opinion.
He said that the minister of health hates genexpert because of their high prices, monopoly, and being expensive to maintain (He is actually an economic guy before becoming minister) 
He suggested that we look into multipanel respiratory diagnostics, because right now none made with TB included. Private hospitals using them and the price isn’t very restricted.
Portnat

They said that portnat test is near completion for phase 1 trial, around 500 samples done from 600ish target.
Portnat is simpler and easier to use compare to pluslife. But not as simple as our workflow.

The sensitivity is not as good as pluslife (on their website their LoD is 400CFU/ml)
During the trial, they said portnat had a problem in the device, so the manufacturer swap the devices mid trial.
Portnat aim to be POC devices, super compact, easy to use.
Interestingly the user still prefer genexpert compared to pluslife or portnat. Because after preparing and inserting the cartridge into the device, there are no more extra steps to do, just wait for result.
Pluslife Info
Government does pluslife trial at Jogja maybe to seek second opinion, it’s done and they already bought 100 pluslife devices to spread in West and Central Java
Prof Bhacti team is dividing the pluslife trial into 3 phase, 1st phase is 600 samples test in their lab, 2nd phase is run the pluslife at puskesmas and compare to genexepert, final phase is send 20 pluslife to 20 puskesmas but not necessarily to compare with other devices, just to see the acceptance of doctors and lab workers.
For 3rd phase Prof Bhacti said that they buy the cartridges themself, with discounted prices
The devices can run without the software, just insert the cartridge and run, there are led light to indicate the result. The device saved result and can be check when connecting to the software.

User can manually insert patient data into the software by comparing to the code at the sticker on cartridge with run history

The pluslife they are using is run on docking station for 5 devices. The laptop only need to connect to the docking station and automatically detect all connected devices.

They said that they still can’t use the device using smartphone yet.
The thermolyse can run 3 samples at once.

Here’s my take on R2D2:
Importing clinical samples regulation needs to be fully understood first, not just the regulation on the importation process. One of the regulations is Permenkes No.85 Tahun 2020
Material Transfer Agreement (MTA) regulation from MoH. 
Since I am in charge as Biosafety Officer in ISO 35001 in Forindo, I am going to bring this topic to the management, risk management meeting (every Thursday). The audience are all high level management in PSL
Step by step according to Permenkes No. 85 Tahun 2020
Foreign TB Sample → Sending Institution Abroad → Indonesian Research Institution (with BSL-3 lab & legal entity) → Ministry of Health Approval → Port Inspection & Quarantine → Research Use under MTA and Ethical Oversight

UKSW discussion
Ibeng, Adit, Bila, Yosi
Sputum sample storage:
Their concern is that most of the samples that we have are being stored in a Transit Storage (outside BSL-3). According to the SOP, the samples only allowed in Transit Storage for a short period of time. And we already way past the temporary time.
The options are:
Unpack the samples in Transit Storage, and keep it inside the BSL-3 facility → the storage inside BSL-3 also limited. And we don’t know when we will use the samples.
Transfer the positive samples and some purulent samples to PSL, and incinerate the rest of negative samples in UKSW.
We have discussed this, and we thought the second option is easier for both parties.
We brought up the idea of buying or getting TB strains and do the research at UKSW. They are very open to the idea. However, getting the strain might be a challenge even for them. They suggest isolating it from the patient samples, then we might get various strains that are common in Indo. This will take quite some time until we can get pure isolates.

3.2 Research Design The research design to be used is descriptive observational, namely research that describes a condition or problem explored through field observations (Sugiyono, 2018). The research subjects are selected using purposive sampling, or sample selection based on specific criteria. The inclusion criteria in this study include:
Research subjects are suspected pulmonary TB patients aged ≥19 years, both male and female,
Research subjects with TB symptoms based on a doctor’s diagnosis. TB symptoms include low-grade fever, cough lasting >2 weeks, weight loss, and night sweats,
Research subjects include both outpatients and inpatients at RSPAW,
Research subjects have obtained TCM GeneXpert MTB Rif Ultra results from sputum or bronchial lavage samples within a 0–7 day range and have not received anti-TB drugs (OAT) during this period.
The exclusion criteria in this study are:
Research subjects fall into the category of MDR-TB or XDR-TB patients,
Subjects unwilling to participate in the study.
The sputum of patients with these symptoms will be examined using TCM GeneXpert MTB Rif/Ultra. This study is single-blinded, meaning that the researchers from the Formulatrix team do not know the patients’ TCM GeneXpert results before examining and releasing the results from the tongue swab.
The number of patients required for this study is 20 patients (with tongue swabs collected twice from each patient), with the sputum TCM results consisting of 10 positive samples and 10 negative samples.

3.3.1. Participant Selection Participants in this study are patients with suspected TB symptoms based on a doctor’s diagnosis at RSPAW. Participants have undergone TCM GeneXpert testing by the RSPAW Research Team but have not received OAT therapy during this period. Before participating, patients will be asked to read and sign an informed consent form stating their willingness to participate in the study. The officer who will collect the tongue swab samples will provide an explanation regarding the procedure to be carried out, the purpose and benefits of the research, as well as the rights and obligations of the patients during the research process.

ASIA ACTUAL
Provide service to register IVD into Indonesian market (through Ministry of Health, e-katalog), ONLY IF we already have the prerequisites as follow:
CSDT/ ASEAN CSDT (Common Submission Dossier Template)
QMS (Quality Management System) → ISO 13485, or CPAKB/GMP if we only plan to sell in Indonesia only.
Formulatrix as Foreign Direct Investment (PMA, Penanaman Modal Asing) and located in bonded zone, the IVD product can fall into two types:
AKD (Domestic Medical Device) → Needs TKDN
AKL (Imported/Overseas Medical Device) → needs additional permit for distribution (IDAK, Ijin Edar Alat Kesehatan)
They also provide Ad Hoc consultation and recommendations to their partners if we need help for the early stages of setting up the company before going to market. Roughly around $300-$400 per hour.
We need to send them our inquiries or process that we need their expertise, before they can give us the estimation of time and fee.

Tius, Sadila, Bila, Adit, Yosi:
Regarding additional PCR data of tongue swab sample, there are two options:
Use their real-time PCR Bioneer.
Pro: we use the same swab sample (from 1 swab)
Cons: open-close tongue swab vials (exposure risk, spill risk); small volume aliquot will be taken from sample, causing different concentrations of direct PCR sample in DPTS vs PCR machine

We offered a self-swab option but they will need one dedicated person to give information and guide the patients to do it. They have a concern that the patient could not perform a proper method of self-swabbing, causing the patient to vomit/cough, and not giving us a good quality of swab samples. also at risk of transmitting to other patients
We were offered to do self-sampling directly to the patient’s house. But in doing so, we were suggested to hire a professional healthcare officer who is trained to take swab samples. There are also some mandatory ethical regulations regarding the sampling procedure to the patient’s residence. e.g:// If we were to visit them by an ambulance, it would cause the patient to feel nervous/ashamed/scared, a certain ethical procedure needs to be done to eliminate this kind of things.

Union conference 2024 Summary (Yosi’s POV)
Detection target
Most TB cases in Indonesia are Rif-sensitive and Inh-resistant.
Iso-marker is important, we can check the WHO database for Indonesia’s mutation
Non MTB detection is also important.
Current GeneXpert testing is not completely fit for testing in all  countries.
IVD tools
Genes2Me: OnePCR – Fully Automated Detection System
Separate reagent well inside one cartridge. Controlled using liquid handling inside the device. Extracted DNA will be transferred to a new vial containing lyop assay, then amplified.
Truenat - Molbio
All consumables were prepared.
New device for sonication, called Truenat lyse. Claimed to be able to process tongue swab samples, but still under validation process.	
Coyote Bioscience
FlashDetect Nano
Sleek looking device, cartridge size similar to genexpert’s. 
Several line detections (respiratory, TB, HPV, HIV, etc)
Intelligenome
Crispr-pcr based diagnostic tools. Still unclear whether it uses blood- or sputum/saliva as the sample. Consumables look similar to a retractable pen. There’s a membrane for catching DNA (incubation 15 min) then reaction with crispr-pcr reagent for another 15 min. Result based on fluorescence intensity
Illumina - Genoscreen
Sequence based analysis to detect 15 antibiotics resistance genes, mixture amplicons. Sequencing after pcr, then detect each antibiotic resistance-related gene.
There are several portable X-ray devices.
Yosi’s impression and questions:
Where do we want to sell Stampede?
If we focus on selling in Indonesia, we might need to alter our target genes. We can add a disclaimer on our product that Stampede is made in Indonesia, designed for Indonesia.
Which market are we targeting?
Screening test - portable, fast, not necessarily need to have too many target genes 
First line Diagnostic test - more comprehensive testing to find first line antibiotics resistance. 
Let’s focus on one key point for our device. More development of additional features for the next version.
Rather than trying to beat other IVD that are on the market, let’s focus on being the best of our version. 
If we keep wanting to pursue technologies currently available on the market, we won’t be able to produce even a single device.
We need to be more active in attending seminars/conferences/training/exhibitions, sending the suitable and relevant people for the occasion.
One idea to speed up development research from the science part, is to collaborate with universities. 
PRO: able to get TB strains (might be easier than we try to bet it by ourself), have more man power to do experiments (from those who are doing their thesis project), experimental results can be published and be a baseline for validation process.
CONS:Higher budget, possibility to have a co-collaborator option (are we ready to share the fame?), might take time to setup from proposal to real experimental stage.

Kalgen meeting topics:
Manufacturing discussion and factory tour. Can they tell us about their reagent manufacturing? What do they make in-house? How long did it take to learn the processes and build the infrastructure?
Regulatory experience? Can they tell us about the process they went through to achieve Ijin Edar for their TB test? Can they give us any advice or connect us with anyone at the national MoH?
Discuss FMLX TB test. Emphasize that our test will not directly compete with them because ours is a point of care test and theirs is a lab test. We can still be friends 🙂.
Once we finish the general discussion about our companies and TB tests, then we can talk about collaboration:
Co-marketing
Kalgen and Formulatrix go to market as partners. “The Kalgen TB Test, powered by Formulatrix.” We would make instruments and cartridges. Kalgen would provide assays, manage regulatory approval, and handle sales and marketing. We would split revenue based on our responsibilities.
Reagent supply
Formulatrix would contract Kalgen as their exclusive or non-exclusive reagent supplier. We could create a supply agreement with a non-binding forecast for a few years. We would pay a fair price to Kalgen for their reagents.
Consulting
Formulatrix would hire Kalgen to give guidance and support to get our TB test onto the market in Indonesia. We would pay a reasonable fee for their time and expertise.
Future assay development
Formulatrix would contract Kalgen scientists to develop new assays for our testing platform. We would pay an up-front fee plus milestone payments for validated assays.

January 24-30, 2024
Draft Feasibility Cooperation with KalGen
Visit from Kalbe (Jan 24, 2024) to PSL
Guest: 
Wahyu Retno Ambarwati (Retno) - Director PT Kalgen DNA
Akterono Dwi Budiyati (Akter) - Team Leader Translational Research IVD Department
Anastasia Cindy Muliawan (Cindy) - Business Development Dept.
Host: Ferry, Bakti, Alek, Yosi
Kalgen DNA is a manufacturing and service company for oncology and infectious disease diagnostic tools.
After we signed the NDA, discussion started with the following general points. 
Kalgen gave information that the government has a program to supply PCR machines to clinics all around Indonesia. The idea is an open-system PCR, which can be used for diagnostic tools of different types of disease. In another program, the government also allocated a budget for TB elimination in Indonesia.
Kalgen heard about the TB IVD device being developed in FMX. Considering FMX is a local company, there is a high possibility that Stampede could be a good option for National TB elimination programs. 
They will send us their TB reagent kit, and we will test it in our device, liquid and dried.
It will be a challenge for them to tweak their reagent properties for our current need (stable in room temperature, the probes’ wavelength). But first, we will test their reagent and we’ll see how it goes.
There was a discussion about the usage of Stampede for HPV detection. In this case, they can start to develop their reagent so it will fit with our requirements for Stampede. In addition, IVD devices for HPV are still rare in Indonesia, there’s no competition yet.
Kalgen already went through the process to “introduce” their kit and IVD instruments to the Ministry of Health. They are very open and willing to guide us so that the Ministry of Health and other correlated institutes would notice FMX and our Stampede.

Waste handling, including toxic and dangerous waste, biohazard waste, and wastewater treatment.

We opened discussion with the presentation on company profile followed by introducing Formulatrix TB test. As an overview, we are stressing on the ability of Stampede, with shorter run time and detection of RIF and INH resistance at the same time.

Discussion Question and Answer
Overview Advantages and TB product
Cycle Time so far we have faster than the current PCR test and Extraction. 
Extraction In chip? They gave a suggestion that if we can simplify the extraction step, that will be a huge advantage over other devices.
Capacity in running sample. More modules is preferable.
Gold standard for detection of RIF and INH. 
Dr Tammy asks how we determine the gold standard, we answer the standard is to match with genexpert’s performance, and if available with TB culture test. She suggests we use a better device than genexpert, since genexpert only detects RIF. She introduces Genoscholar, which detects both RIF and INH. Disadvantage of Genoscholar is that the extraction and PCR process is separate. It only detects the amplification of each target and translates it to a strip band.
ISO 14155
Dr. Tammy also enlightened us about ISO 14155, which was already adapted by Indonesia's MoH to be a guideline book. MoH also hosted a seminar about ISO 14155 last December 2022, which regulates clinical research on medical devices, including pre-clinical and clinical trials. Dr. Irandi suggested that we speak to a consultant about this regulation.
UI Hospital has 9 lung specialist doctors, each having 4-5 patients a day.

Scenario Plan For Clinical research
MTB RESEARCH=> This is more familiar with current medical faculty or some specialist MTB doctors research, so they research from the mutations or some methods of culture on plate or some research to identify the treatment or drug or observation (some rontgen and AI to decide positif result) on the MTB patient. The scope is much wider in MTB.
Medical Device Research => is what we need. I guess we only need the data comparison with the culture TB or current diagnostic tools (IVD). so if I take a looks in the current status collaborator and permitted or regulation what we have know are

UKSW=> have the BSL3 and granted the Medical Faculty in 2023 (we build relation in end 2022) the other concern they don’t have yet any experience on the Medical research but contributed on Covid 19 testing in Salatiga. So we start building from just talking from zero. They also want the detail of the organization of our activity. 


Dinas Kesehatan (MoH Salatiga) the scope is only in the Salatiga city.  They help us to obtain permits to get access to  TB patients from Public healthcare (Puskesmas) in Salatiga. The regulations they follow is the Mayor or governor of Salatiga city. The smallest layers in the MoH. So far their main activity is to push down some diseases and make sure all activity in the city is safe for the healthy. Now we have the approval to get the real sample from Puskesmas.with in the procedure that we applied in the past is not ready yet to follow GCP correctly. Still based on what we know from learning itself. 
Our concern here is we worry if they have coordination with the Java district  or Indonesian MoH will have another regulation or not the same as city MoH. 
RSPAW hospital, in the past we have done some Initial study on their labs observing how to know in the hospitals and we purpose clinical activity and they want we have a GCP certificate on the researcher from our Formulatrix site. and also they want to know the ethical clearance for current activity already done or not (on that discussion we are not yet know & submit it). The hospital has a direct command from the Head of Ministry of Health of Indonesia. 
GCP does not yet have (plan on september) and we will implement the standard or procedure during all activities in Salatiga. And also if we want to go to another University as previously we have an initial discussion with the University of Indonesia.
University of Indonesia , The initial discussion we have with the Head of TB research department, they have a MTB research on their Hospital and this university has a big chance to match with scenario Medical device research only above. We get a lesson on how to do clinical tests. They also have experience with MoH of Jakarta where it is hard to get permits to do MTB research. It is impressive that we can go through the Salatiga MoH but it looks weird because the standard is clear what we should have to do in GCP content but we pass the permit in salatiga. We need to have more discussion and collect the feasibility and lessons of the current systems or regulation research in indonesia.

Design review for USB-C power delivery, ultrasonic inverter board, and ultrasonic matching impedance board.
The design review is already done, the PCBs are placed to order.

Sonicator Driver: Increase transformer ratio.
The purpose of increasing the transformer in this test is to know the effect on sonication and what happens if the ratio is increased . In this experiment, we did a modification of the existing transformer by removing the secondary winding and recreating it with higher turns. The existing transformer currently used for our driver has primary winding 25 for each to center tap, and secondary winding 60 turns. For the new winding we create 90 turns so the transformer ratio changes from 1:2.5 to 1:3.6.

Fig 1. Rewinding process. Primary winding. New Secondary winding.

We can calculate the number of turns by using:
,
The inductance factor (Al) for the transformer is 6.2uH.
Result:
After the transformer finishes assembly, we test it first by using a signal generator to see the voltage amplification on each frequency and the result is good, starting from 20KHz until 50KHz the voltage amplification is flat 3.6x.
Then test it with the driver using an old matching impedance network (L matching). The result is not good, when the driver is set to resonance frequency, the transformer produces a ringing sound and the waveform is not stable.
Video tests.
It also found that the current draw from the power supply increased drastically until the power supply changed to constant current and the voltage drop.

Fig 2. 1:3.6 transformer test on the driver.
The cavitation still occurs but not optimum caused by an unstable voltage source because of the power supply switch to constant current mode.
Analysis:
For the transformer, if there is a load on the connected secondary side, the primary side will not see the same impedance value. The primary side sees the load through the lens of the transformer turns ratio.
If  is the ratio of primary secondary transformer:
, then the impedance seen by the primary side is:

For 2.5 ratio,
,
For 3.6 ration,

For the 2.5 ratio, the current drawn from the power supply is 4.5A the impedance seen by the primary is approximately 2.67 Ohm mean while on the secondary side the impedance is 16.68 Ohm. When the transformer was changed to 1:3.6, the impedance seen by the primary side now drop to 0.771 * 16.68 = 1.28Ohm. With the same matching configuration, the power supply should provide around 9.37A current to handle this load. Because the existing power supply can only provide 5.7A max, that's why the power supply drops the voltage and enters constant current mode.
In the other things, maybe the imperfect winding process also affects the transformer performance. 

To answer the curiosity, then we tried to use the handheld transformer that has 1:4 ratio but with better wire winding and it also uses a litz wire.

Fig 3. Use handheld ultrasonic transformer drivers with 1:4 turns ratio.

Results:
The power supply still can enter constant current, but with careful tuning of the circuit, the waveform can be a little bit stable and can produce good sonication.


Fig 4. Testing 1:4 transformer from handheld driver
It also has been tested with the temperature increase. Even though the power supply keeps dropping, increasing the ratio can increase the temperature Ramp Up if compared with 1:2.5 ratio. Even the difference is not so much, but this is a sign that the transformer ratio can help to increase the force or velocity of transducer vibration the same as if we increase the power supply voltage.

Fig 5. Temperature increases in comparison.
For 1:2.5 ratio, temperature ramp up is 1.58C/s, max temperature for 25 cycles is 67.3C.
For 1:4 ratio, temperature ramp up is 1.65C/s, maximum temperature for 25 cycles is 70.3C.
For the excitation voltage, since the transformer ratio was increased, the excitation voltage should be increased. But because at this test the power supply enters constant current and the voltage drops, so the excitation voltage is a little bit the same with the 1:2.5 ratio.

One more thing we got from this test is that the impedance on the secondary side when the driver is set close to resonance is quite small, 16.8 Ohms. But the question is, is 16.8 Ohm the real impedance or dominated by the imaginary part ?
Because we are missing the phase shift data between voltage and current, so are not yet can be found in which area this impedance belongs to ?

To analyze the phase margin, we plan to use zero crossing to understand the phase margin between current and voltage.
 In a Resonant Converter design (like the one you created), we are not looking for a 0 phase between the voltage peak and the current peak, but rather ZVS (Zero Current Switching) or ZCS (Zero Current Switching). 
ZCS (Zero Current Switching): we must ensure that the zero current point (when the current changes from negative to positive) occurs exactly at the voltage transition from the bottom box to the top box. If the current reaches zero before the voltage transition, our system is capacitive (leading). If the current reaches zero after the transition, our system is inductive (lagging).

Experiment with alternative matching impedance network for reliable sonicator driver.
While supporting seno to debug the device-006, we also did some experiment on the sonicator driver by modifying the matching impedance network that might help to improve the sonication reliability.
Current impedance matching network for the driver is called L matching, which consists of a series inductor and parallel with the compensation capacitor. It only has one voltage magnification at the cutoff frequency (the highest Q), below or higher than the resonant and cutoff frequency, the impedance will shift (dominated by inductive or capacitive).

Fig 1. Current L matching impedance network.
The L match is the simplest matching network with enough sonication performance. But this matching network only has 1 center frequency and the bandwidth is narrow. To remember the L matching performance, here is the temperature increase and ramp-up at the testing:
Ramp-up 1.56C/s.

Fig 2. L Matching impedance network temperature increase.

To find another better impedance matching, we already did some experiments to add another matching network together with the L matching. The alternative matching networks are called:
LCCL matching. Consists of L series, C parallel, C series, and L parallel. The purpose of adding C series and L parallel is to create second resonant and cut-off frequencies to have wider bandwidth.
LCCCL matching. Consists of L series paralleled with C (LC series), C parallel, C series, and L parallel. Adding paralleled C on the L series is to create anti resonance after higher cutoff frequency to remove third harmonic and later that may still exist.
Detailed results can be seen here.

LCCL.
Compared to L matching, this network still has lower performance based on cavitation and temperature increase even though the current is almost the same with L matching. Video.

Fig 3. LCCL matching impedance and its response (simulation)


Fig 4. LCCL cavitation and temperature ramp-up compared to L matching
LCCCL.
Compared to L matching, this network has a little bit higher cavitation performance and temperature ramp-up. 

Fig 5. LCCCL network and its response (simulation)


Fig 6. LCCCL cavitation and temperature ramp-up compared to L matching and LCCL
Based on the result, the alternative matching impedance network still not yet shows better performance, it's maybe because the component value still not yet optimally chooses. And also, in the last experiment, we found that the LCCL and LCCCL somehow didn’t work and seemed to have very low cavitation. I need to check on the driver (MOSFET, Trafo, and the inductor) . Maybe one of them was broken.

TPS2492 Hot-Swap Controller for 20V Type-C Powered System with Inrush Current Limiting 
Measured system behavior shows:
Peak inrush current: 27.7 A
Initial spike duration: 10 µs
Maximum inrush window: up to 500 µs
Input supply: 20V Type-C adapter with overcurrent protection at 5A
To ensure reliable startup without triggering adapter protection, a hot-swap controller was implemented using the Texas Instruments TPS2492. This device enables controlled inrush current limiting, programmable fault timeout, and safe MOSFET ramp-up for capacitive loads and switching regulator inputs.
Current Limiter
The TPS2492 monitors the voltage drop across an external current sense resistor connected between the source of the MOSFET and the load return path. When the voltage across RSENSE reaches the internal threshold:
VSENSE(th) = 50 mV (typical)
the controller enters current regulation mode.In this mode:
The IC reduces MOSFET gate drive
The MOSFET operates in linear region
Load current is clamped at a constant value
Inrush current is actively limited
Adapter Type C (Max.5A), ILimit  target (safe) = 4.5 A so Vsense = 45 mV
ILimit = Vsense / RSense
4.5A = 45 mV / RSense
RSense = 0.01 Ω 
RSENSE (mΩ)
	ILIMIT (Approx.)
	Risk to 5A Adapter (OCP)
	Engineering Note
	
5 mΩ
	9–10 A
	Adapter will definitely trip
	Excessive inrush, not suitable for Type-C current-limited supply
	
8 mΩ
	6 A
	High risk of OCP triggering
	May still exceed adapter protection threshold
	
10 mΩ
	4.5–5 A
	Safest / most balanced choice
	Good balance between fast startup and adapter protection margin
	
15 mΩ
	3 A
	Very safe for adapter
	Startup ramp becomes slower due to stronger current limiting
	

Fault Timeout (C Timer)
The CTIMER pin defines the fault timeout duration. This means: How long the IC allows an overcurrent or current-limiting condition before it is considered a fault and the device shuts down. CTIMER mainly determines:
The allowed time during input capacitor charging (inrush phase).
The tolerance time before shutdown if the current is still in the current limit.
Ctotal on the SAMBo is about 1036 UF (Motor Drivers, buck converters, Hotswap output). 







 1.3 to 2   → Min.safe 6 ms
Formula (on the datasheet):
VTH = 1.23V
ITIMER= 50 µA



 (Nearest standard value→ 220nF or 270nF).

02/10/2026
Debug SAMBo v3 USB-C power triggered over current protection.
During test v3 system 1, we found that using a USB-C power adapter when the power push button pushed, the adapter suddenly tripped and showed an over current indicator on the LED. This issue happens on all of the USB-C power adapters, but not on the power bank battery. We check there is no short circuit, and the load without the motor should be less than 1A. We suspect that there is a high inrush current on the board, based on the last experience we had a few half ago, the inrush current was caused by many bulk capacitors on the board. From the measurement using a current probe, when the power button is pressed, the current increases to 27.7A @10uS and keeps high up to 500uS. This duration (500uS) is enough for the USB-C power adapter to detect the over current and trigger the protection.

Fig 1. Power test
The root cause based on the last same issue, is there are big capacitors in the load side, and it causes a high current draw during initial capacitor charge. 

Fig 2. Bulk capacitor on the motor driver
The bulk capacitor has a purpose to reduce the dI/dt when the motor energizes by storing some energy in the capacitor and it will provide the current for a short period of time and will not draw high current directly to the adapter. But, on the other hand it will cause high inrush current and if it is seen by the adapter it will trigger the over current protection in the power adapter.
To mitigate this problem, first we remove the 1x 330uF capacitors, remove 2x 300uS:
The removed 1x 330uF capacitor still has the same inrush current in the same duration and still causes a power trip on the USB-C adapter. Removed 2x 330uF capacitors reduce the inrush current at the same duration and don’t cause power trip.

Fig 3. Left: removed 1x 330uF capacitor, Right: removed 2x 330uF capacitors

Here is the inrush current difference before removing the capacitor, remove 1x 330uF capacitor, remove 2x 330uF capacitor.




Fig 4. Top: 2x 330uF exists, mid: removed 1x 330uF, bottom: removed 2x 330uF capacitors


The 330uF is needed to maintain dI/dt keep lower during motor driver draw current, if we remove this capacitor, it might create another issue regarding higher dI/dT. Because the issue is power trip on the adapter, we need to decrease the high inrush current when the power button is pressed. It means that, before supplying current to the main board, it should provide enough energy for some short period to minimize direct current draw from the power adapter. The power distribution is divided into 3 stages:

The USB-C PD board will request the voltage and max current to the power adapter, if requested power is available, the power adapter will give the output. From this diagram, the USB-C power delivery board should provide enough current for a short period of time by placing some bulk capacitor to avoid direct high current draw to the power adapter, so the dI/dT and the inrush current can be minimized. After take a look on the power delivery board schematic, there is only 1 22uF (C1) capacitor on the MOSFET output:
 
Because on the mainboard power controller, the bulk capacitor is placed after P-Channel mosfet, before button pressed the bulk capacitor charge is still empty, after button pressed the power module(USB-C PD) should supply an amount of current to charge the bulk capacitor on the mainboard. Because there is only 1 capacitor (22uF) on the USB-C PD, it is not enough to provide the needed charge from the mainboard so the USB-C module will draw a lot of current from the power adapter to provide the needed charge for the mainboard for a short period of time. This timeline will generate high inrush current and then be detected by the power adapter and trigger the power adapter protection. By this, we can put a bigger capacitor on the USB-C to provide an amount of charge. If the mainboard requests a short period for charging its bulk capacitor, the power module (USB-C) still has enough charge without drawing large current from the power adapter.
After adding 1x 330uF on the USB-C power delivery out, now the mainboard can turn on normally and the inrush current is not detected by the current probe and also the power adapter didn’t trigger the over current protection.


Fig 5. Add 1 bulk capacitor and test to the system
Another advanced solution is using efuse, and we already test using TPS16530PWPR. It can reduce the inrush current much and also have an over current protection and over temperature protection. Using the efuse might also eliminate traditional fuse.
Debug Sonicator Driver.
In this test we are still curious why the feedback signal from the current transformer doesn't have the same phase to the excitation voltage even the frequency and matching network close to the transducer resonance frequency. 
If we take a look at the method to measure the resonance frequency using the impedance bridge, when the frequency is matched with the transducer frequency, the output signal will be the same phase from signal input.
 
But if we compare the excitation voltage with the current sensed by the current transformer, it seems that the phase margin stays the same even if it was resonant or out of resonance. Only current amplitude will change if the frequency is resonant or not. 

Fig 1. Left: Out of resonance, right: in resonance.
We suspect that, maybe it is because of a transformer present on the driver, so the leakage inductance and the parasitic capacitance cause this. If so, the phase still should be shifted if the frequency shifted or changed. To find the clue, we tried to measure the current from the power supply not on the transducer. We also removed the current feedback on the driver and manually tuning the potentiometer to find the correct frequency.

The result is, the phase shift between excitation voltage and the current is the same with the feedback to excitation.


Fig 2. Left: frequency far from resonance (incorrect matching net). Right: frequency near with the resonance frequency (almost correct matching net)
From this result, we know that the comparison between excitation voltage and the current (from transducer or power supply) have the same phase margin along the frequency. If we take a look again on the impedance bridge, the voltage signal is the source signal from the signal generator. But in our case, the excitation signal is converted from square wave into sine wave by accumulating energy on the inductance and capacitance + transducer and creating LC series resonance. And thus sine wave is not the source voltage signal if we want to compare with the current. The source signal itself is the square wave from the generator. So from this we should compare the current waveform with the square wave as the voltage to know the phase margin in every frequency.
Then we tried to measure again, and observe the phase margin between square wave signal and current waveform from the current transformer.

Fig 3. Left: On resonance frequency, Right: off resonance frequency
If we compare the current waveform to square wave, there is a phase shift on the several frequencies. But to determine the phase shift, because it is a different waveform, we need to know how to measure the phase shift between the square wave and the sine wave. Or if we can modulate the square wave in tho sine wave without distorting the phase it might be easy to measure the phase shift.

Planar Transformer For Sonicator Driver.
We got an interesting idea to use the Planar Transformer that was presented by Asfan in this document. The key feature of this transformer are:
Small dimension can save the PCB layout area
High efficiency
Suitable for high frequency switching
Low noise level by minimizing the electromagnetic interference generated during operation.
Production scale

Fig 1. Example of planar transformer
Key parameters for designing and implementing planar transformer:
The operating frequency for the sonicator is 30 - 40KHz, these frequencies are below the common planar transformer application use (>100KHz). To optimize the use of planar t
transformer, we need to successfully determine some parameters below:
Core material
Number of turns
Maximum flux density (BMax) to avoid high temperature generated
Skin depth, Trace dimension, and Proximity effect.

Core material.
The core selection based on its application has a purpose to avoid core saturation due to high flux density caused by high inductance.
 				(1)
 			(2)
If the L is too high caused by the material permeability (µ), the flux density (B) will dramatically increase even with a small amount of current (I). Once the B reaches the limit of material, the core will be saturated immediately.
Based on this document, the material that is suitable for switching transformers (planar or traditional) is the R, K or J material code ferrite core. To prevent core saturation while maintaining an efficient magnetizing current, the core's initial permeability () must be carefully selected. A value between 2000 – 3000 is ideal for power applications with switching frequencies above 20kHz, as it offers a balanced trade-off between high flux density capacity and low core losses
Number of turns and maximum flux density (BMax).
This parameter will determine the maximum flux density to choose the correct core to make sure that the core of the Bmax is still far enough from the core Bsaturation. The BMax can be calculated as:
		(3), for sinusoidal wave and
			(4), for square waves.
For  in  commonly mentioned in datasheet, we can multiple the nominator with .
Skin depth, Trace dimension, and Proximity effect.
Skin depth or skin effect is a phenomenon where the current flows relatively only on the surface to some depth of the conductor. In sonicator application, we could calculate the skin depth for some frequencies range and use correct conductor thickness on the PCB.
			(5), for copper conductor material it can be simplified by
				(6)
For 20KHz frequency, the skin depth would be:
 from the copper surface.
For 40KHz frequency, the skin depth would be:
 from the copper surface.
This number is very safe for implementation on the sonicator driver. When ordering the PCB we can choose 1oz copper thickness because it only has 35um thickness and the current will flow in the entire copper.
Trace dimension should be considered based on the maximum temperature increase depending on the power needed. We can choose 2oz for a high power application sonicator. The clearance between traces should be considered if the switching voltage is quite high.
The main concern on this planar design is the proximity effect. The current on the one layer will repel current in the other layer so it will increase the AC resistance. To avoid this we should design the planar transformer with interleaved stack for primary and secondary. The best practice is : pri (top layer)-sec (inner layer 1)-pri (inner layer 2)-sec (inner layer 3)-pri (inner layer 4)-sec (inner layer 5) - pri (bottom layer). This interleaving layer stacking will cancel out the magnetic field on each layer then will decrease the proximity effect and the leakage inductance.

Design.
In the latest sonicator design, the current (I) peak estimated from average DC current power supply, efficiency, and duty cycle is 4A @12V. 
We need to calculate the flux density estimated by using the current and voltage from the latest testing and use some value of inductance and Ae. Once we get the Bmax, we can choose the core that is still far enough from our estimated BMax. Or we can choose first the core, and calculate the L to keep far away from core B saturation.
During the core sourcing, there is no Bsat parameter mentioned on the datasheet.
Let assume the core sat is 0.5T (500mT). We want to set the desired flux density during the sonication process is 100 - 200 mT. This range to avoid the core losses and minimize the heat generated during sonication. We choose the core with different dimensions and the effective cross section is (Ae).
Core A: Datasheet.
	 
	
Core B: Datasheet

Both of the cores produce a small number of turns (N). This will introduce inefficiency due to the higher current magnetization and the driver will seem to drive high power even the sonicator not on the resonance frequency. Other than that, lower number of turns and inductance will cause extreme sensitivity to DC offset that will lead the core easily to reach saturation.
To accommodate this, we need to decrease the inductance factor (AL) on both of the cores so the number of turns will increase and minimize the early core saturation at higher current. With the same core, we need to add a small gap to produce lower AL and add the air gap as a magnetic energy reservoir and air can’t be saturated.

Prototyping ZTP-315 infrared amphenol temperature sensor.
The sensor is already received, and we start making the signal conditioning. The output of the sensor is voltage relative to the temperature and not very linear. The voltage level is very small, at 80 C, the output voltage is around 11mVolt. The maximum remote sensing is 120C. To make it can be read by the microcontroller ADC, it needs to be amplified. Datasheet.
This sensor also integrated with an internal NTC thermistor to sense the ambient temperature to compensate for the IR temperature reading at the calculation.

Fig 1. Sensor and its response

The amplification can be set to match with the maximum ADC voltage, for example if we need to make the 80 deg have a 3.3V output we can use amplification 300x. In this test we use the instrument amplifier INA826 and set the gain around 100x to avoid noise because we use a prototype board. 

Fig 2. Sensor signal conditioning
The signal conditioning has 2 outputs, 1 for thermopile output (infrared sensor), and the second is thermistor for ambient temperature sensor.

For this prototype we use the old stampede controller board that has the INA826 amplifier, and utilize STM32 for the data acquisition.


Fig 3. Prototype setup and sensor attachment on the testing JIG
Temperature conversion from the electrical.
Because this sensor does not have a linear response, to convert the electrical value to the temperature value it can be used a Stefan-Boltzmann equation. For thermopile sensor:

Q = is the heat flux
 = Emissivity
 = Stefan-Boltzmann constant
T = absolute temperature.
For the IR thermopile sensor like ZTP-315 the equation can be rewritten as:

, where
V = voltage output
K = Calibration constant
T = absolute temperature.
To find the value of K we can use some mathematical calculations from voltage output based on some temperature point. In this prototype we refer to this reference and use its K value and apply it to our system. Reference doc.

Fig 4. Variation of K value and its error response.

Testing.
In this prototype the objective is :
To see if the red light LED from the vial heater affect the IR reading or not
How much temperature difference is sensed from the outside vial compared to temperature inside the vial sensed by the thermocouple.

1. Red light LED effect.
The sensor has the infrared wave length response from 5um to 16um

Fig 5. ZTP-315 wave length response vs vial heater LED wave length.
From this data, the LED light shouldn’t affect the IR temperature reading because the wavelength response from the IR sensor is very far compared to the LED red light wavelength. From the testing, it proved that the LED light didn’t affect the IR reading. When the LED OFF the IR sensor reading around 27C, after LED turned ON there is no jumping signal on the temperature reading, and the temperature reading will increase gradually based on the vial temperature during the LED stay ON. Video.
 


Fig 6. LED light didn’t affect the temperature reading.
2. Temperature difference is sensed from the outside vial compared to temperature inside the vial sensed by the thermocouple
In this test we found that the sensor positioning also affects the temperature reading, if the sensor position is not sensing in front of the liquid, the temperature difference compared to the thermocouple is around 25C.


Fig 7. IR sensor position on the top of liquid surface

If the sensor position is in the liquid area, the temperature difference between the IR sensor and the thermocouple is around 5C


Fig 8. IR sensor position on the liquid area
By this, we can see that the IR sensor can be used for sensing the liquid temperature for heating the vial because the LED light didn’t affect the temperature reading by the IR, but further testing should be done to get a good sensor positioning, calculate the K value to have good sensing precision.

Other next to do is testing together with the vial cap heater and do some bang bang testing controller by temperature data and compare the result with the old method that uses a load cell data for bang bang controller.

The RD3.0.0 board has already arrived and finished from soldering. Have an issue with the inductor production, 2 of 7 inductors produced arcing and plasma when the driver turned on and caused transducer failure and getting hot.

Fig 1. Inductor produce an arcing and plasma smell
Our assumption is that there is a part of the litz wires that the insulator was damaged, when high voltage occurs it will create a corona discharge and disturb the magnetic field inside the inductor core. Then we disassemble the inductor to add additional varnish (corona dope) to ensure that all of the litz wire is covered by this insulator.
 
Fig 2. Pouring the inductor winding with additional a corona dope varnish
After waiting around 24 hours for curing, then assemble the inductor with the core and test using LCR meter and in the driver.
One of the inductors seems to be shorted because when measured with the LCR meter its shows only 9uH (should be 2mH) and the other one the inductance value is still fine.

Fig 3. Test 2 inductors with LCR meter after adding additional insulator varnish.
But when tested on the driver, it seems like the inductor can’t transfer the current to the transducer and the waveform outputs on the excitation voltage only square wave + harmonic.

Fig 4. Test 2 inductors with the driver after adding additional insulator varnish.
So by this we assume that there are many layers of winding that might have an issue with the litz wire insulation and adding the additional varnish corona dope didn’t help it to cover the damaged insulator. For now we have 4 working inductors and will produce another inductor for sparepart.

This phenomena is still under debugging, because increasing a capacitor will lower the total impedance of the burden. If we follow the ohm law where V = I * Z, the output voltage should be lower if Z is decreasing.




But there will be another analysis, the Z itself will not be pure resistive. If we calculate the Z based on the RC parallel we can get:




But if we consider the leakage inductance, the impedance and phase shift now will be affected by the inducatnce.



Where the XC is the capacitive reactance. If we take an example, if the C is 1nF at 35KHz frequency the reactance will be around 6KOhm, and if we increase the capacitance to 100nF the  reactance will be around 63KOhm. At 1nF the Z will be dominated by the R burden, but at the 100nF capacitance the Z will be affected by the reactive and resistive and thus will affect the phase as well.

Decreasing the transformer ratio can reduce the current draw since the reflected secondary side impedance to the primary side is lower and vice versa. So by this, there are 2 assumption :
We must set the reflected secondary side impedance in correct value, so the power and mechanical conversion could be effective
Too or too high reflected impedance will sacrifice the electro-mechanical conversion.
Temperature ramp-up plot for each configuration with measurement setup:
Duty : 0.5s
Off delay: 1s
Cycle count: 25
Data here.

Fig 2. Temperature ramp-up plot
From those plots, assumption 2 may be correct because the lower and higher transformer ratio take the lower ramp-up rate. 
From here, we still not can determine the correct transformer ratio and inductor inductance value but we will the highest (2mH L and 3.06 Ratio) and the middle (3mH L and 3.06Ratio or 2mH L and 2.12 Ratio), we will continue test and also fishing the JIG first due the sonicator tip was not perpendicular to the vial.

For IDEA 3, in the current driver circuit we can’t do a duty cycle adjustment because the driver uses a push-pull topology. We need to modify the driver to use full bridge topology and change the gate driver so the duty cycle can be applied to the circuit.
